topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL308 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUIT AND LOGIC DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** RC Integrating and Differentiating Circuits (Transient Analysis with Different Inputs and Frequency Response)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **RC Integrating Circuit** is a passive linear time-invariant network consisting of a resistor ($R$) and a capacitor ($C$) in series, where the **output voltage is taken across the capacitor**. The circuit performs a mathematical integration operation on the input signal when the time constant $\tau = RC$ is very large compared to the period ($T$) of the input waveform. Mathematically, the output approximates:

$$V_{out}(t) \approx \frac{1}{RC}\int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau)\, d\tau$$

An **RC Differentiating Circuit** is the dual configuration where the **output voltage is taken across the resistor** and the time constant $\tau = RC$ is very small compared to the period of the input. It performs a mathematical differentiation operation:

$$V_{out}(t) \approx RC\,\frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The *transient analysis* portion of this experiment tests the student's ability to plot the step response, square-wave response, and triangular-wave response of both circuits. The *frequency response* portion tests the Bode-plot interpretation, the corner frequency $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$, and the classification of the circuits as **first-order Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** and **first-order High-Pass Filter (HPF)** respectively.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

### The "Bucket and Hose" Analogy (Integrator)
Imagine a **water tank (capacitor)** being filled by a hose whose flow rate is controlled by a **valve (resistor)**. The rate at which water enters the tank is proportional to the pressure difference between the hose and the tank's water level. The tank's water level is the *integral* of the inflow over time. The resistor restricts (controls) the current, while the capacitor accumulates (integrates) the charge. This is exactly how an RC integrator works — current is the flow, and voltage across the capacitor is the accumulated level.

### The "Speed Bump" Analogy (Differentiator)
Now imagine a **speedometer (resistor = measuring element)** mounted on a car. The voltage across this element is proportional to the *rate of change* of position. If the car cruises at a constant speed, the speedometer reads zero change; but during sudden acceleration or braking, the reading spikes. The capacitor blocks DC and only allows *changes* to pass through, so the resistor "sees" only the rapid variations — this is differentiation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The exact same RC network can act as either an integrator OR a differentiator. The difference lies in **two design choices**: (1) Where you tap the output (across $C$ or across $R$), and (2) The relationship between $\tau = RC$ and the period $T$ of the input signal.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following fundamental quantities govern the operation of these circuits and must be memorized for KTU lab examinations:

- **Time constant:** $\tau = RC$ (measured in seconds, denoted with the bold symbol $\mathbf{\tau}$)
- **Corner frequency (Cut-off):** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ (measured in **Hertz, Hz**)
- **Angular corner frequency:** $\omega_c = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ (measured in **radians/second, rad/s**)
- **Input pulse width constraint (Integrator):** $T \ll \tau$, i.e., $RC \geq 10T$
- **Input pulse width constraint (Differentiator):** $T \gg \tau$, i.e., $RC \leq 0.1T$
- **Standard component values:** $R = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$ (giving $\tau = 100\,\mu\text{s}$)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

### The Universal RC Series Network
Both circuits share the same fundamental topology. Let $V_{in}(t)$ be the input applied across the series combination of $R$ and $C$, with the loop current $i(t)$ flowing through both elements. By **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)**:

$$V_{in}(t) = V_R(t) + V_C(t)$$

Using the current-voltage relationships of the passive elements:

$$V_{in}(t) = R\,i(t) + \frac{1}{C}\int_{0}^{t} i(\tau)\,d\tau$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to time and substituting $i(t) = C\,\dfrac{dV_C}{dt}$:

$$RC\,\frac{dV_C}{dt} + V_C(t) = V_{in}(t)$$

This is the **first-order linear ordinary differential equation (ODE)** that universally describes the RC circuit. The output is either $V_C(t)$ (integrator) or $V_R(t) = V_{in}(t) - V_C(t)$ (differentiator).

---

### Logic Flow for the Integrator ($V_{out} = V_C$)

1. The time constant $\tau = RC$ is chosen to be **much larger** than the input period $T$.
2. During each half-cycle, the capacitor can only charge/discharge to a small fraction of the full input amplitude.
3. The voltage across the capacitor therefore changes **linearly** with time (since the current $i$ is approximately constant when $\Delta V_C \ll V_{in}$).
4. Since the change in $V_C$ over a half-period $T/2$ is small, we can approximate the current as $i \approx V_{in}/R$.
5. Substituting: $V_C(t) = \dfrac{1}{C}\int i\,dt \approx \dfrac{1}{RC}\int V_{in}\,dt$.
6. The output is a **ramp/triangular waveform** for a square-wave input, or a **shifted sinusoid** for a sinusoidal input.

### Logic Flow for the Differentiator ($V_{out} = V_R$)

1. The time constant $\tau = RC$ is chosen to be **much smaller** than the input period $T$.
2. The capacitor charges almost instantly to the applied voltage (very small $\tau$).
3. Most of the input voltage drops across the resistor as a brief transient.
4. The output waveform for a square wave consists of **sharp positive and negative spikes** (exponential decays) at each transition edge.
5. For a sinusoidal input, the output is a **cosine-shifted sinusoid** scaled by $\omega RC$.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Integrator (LPF) | Differentiator (HPF) |
|---|----------|------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Output Terminal** | Across Capacitor $C$ | Across Resistor $R$ |
| 2 | **Time Constant Condition** | $\tau \gg T$ (i.e., $RC \geq 10T$) | $\tau \ll T$ (i.e., $RC \leq T/10$) |
| 3 | **Differential Equation** | $RC\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt} + V_{out} = V_{in}$ | $RC\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt} + V_{out} = RC\dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ |
| 4 | **Transfer Function** | $H(s) = \dfrac{1}{1+sRC}$ | $H(s) = \dfrac{sRC}{1+sRC}$ |
| 5 | **Frequency Response** | $H(j\omega) = \dfrac{1}{1+j\omega RC}$ | $H(j\omega) = \dfrac{j\omega RC}{1+j\omega RC}$ |
| 6 | **Magnitude** | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1+(\omega RC)^2}}$ | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1+(\omega RC)^2}}$ |
| 7 | **Phase** | $\phi = -\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$ | $\phi = 90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$ |
| 8 | **Corner Frequency** | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ |
| 9 | **Behaviour at $f \ll f_c$** | Passes signal (gain $\approx 1$) | Attenuates signal (gain $\approx \omega RC$) |
| 10 | **Behaviour at $f \gg f_c$** | Attenuates at $-20$ dB/decade | Passes signal (gain $\approx 1$) |
| 11 | **Filter Classification** | First-Order **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** | First-Order **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** |
| 12 | **Step Response Rise/Fall** | Exponential, time constant $\tau = RC$ | Initial spike then exponential decay to zero |
| 13 | **Square-Wave Response** | Triangular wave (ramp up/down) | Sequence of positive/negative spikes |
| 14 | **Sinusoidal Response** | Amplitude-scaled, phase-shifted cosine | Cosine waveform (90° lead), amplitude scaled |
| 15 | **Energy Storage** | Energy **stored** in $C$ (integrating) | Energy **dissipated** in $R$ during transient |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL Markdown Rule Applied:** All absolute value and evaluation bars in the table above use the LaTeX commands `\vert` and `\dfrac` to prevent breaking the pipe-delimited markdown table structure.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Analog Computing** | Solving differential equations in pre-digital era analog computers |
| **Pulse Shaping** | Generating triangular waves from square waves in function generators (integrator) |
| **Edge Detection** | Detecting sharp transitions in digital signals and noise spikes (differentiator) |
| **Filter Design** | First-stage of higher-order active Butterworth and Chebyshev filters |
| **Communication** | FM demodulation, where frequency deviation is converted to amplitude via differentiator |
| **Signal Conditioning** | Removing high-frequency noise (integrator) or DC offset (differentiator) in sensors |
| **Oscilloscope Probes** | Compensating attenuator probes use RC networks to maintain flat frequency response |
| **Biomedical** | ECG/EEG signal processing for isolating QRS complex (differentiation) or baseline drift (integration) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Integrator Response to a Square-Wave Input

**Given:** An RC circuit with $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$ (so $\tau = RC = 1\,\text{ms}$). The input is a square wave of amplitude $V_p = 5\,\text{V}$ and period $T = 100\,\mu\text{s}$.

**Condition Check:** $\tau = 1\,\text{ms}$ and $T = 100\,\mu\text{s}$. Therefore $\tau = 10T$, satisfying $\tau \gg T$. The circuit operates as an integrator.

**Step 1: General Solution of the First-Order ODE**

The governing ODE is:

$$RC\,\frac{dV_C(t)}{dt} + V_C(t) = V_{in}(t)$$

For a constant input $V_{in} = V_p$ during the interval $0 \leq t \leq T/2$, the general solution is the sum of the **steady-state (particular) solution** and the **transient (homogeneous) solution**:

$$V_C(t) = V_C^{ss}(t) + V_C^{tr}(t)$$

**Step 2: Particular Solution (Forced Response)**

At steady state, $\dfrac{dV_C}{dt} = 0$, so the ODE reduces to:

$$V_C^{ss} = V_{in} = V_p = 5\,\text{V}$$

**Step 3: Homogeneous Solution (Natural Response)**

The homogeneous equation $RC\dfrac{dV_C}{dt} + V_C = 0$ has the characteristic equation:

$$RC\,s + 1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = -\frac{1}{RC} = -\frac{1}{\tau}$$

The natural response is therefore:

$$V_C^{tr}(t) = A\,e^{-t/\tau}$$

where $A$ is determined by the initial condition.

**Step 4: Applying Initial Condition**

At $t = 0$, assume the capacitor is uncharged: $V_C(0) = 0$. Therefore:

$$V_C(0) = V_p + A = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = -V_p = -5\,\text{V}$$

**Step 5: Complete Solution for First Half-Cycle**

$$V_C(t) = V_p - V_p\,e^{-t/\tau} = V_p\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

**Step 6: Linearization Using the Condition $\tau \gg T$**

Since $\tau = 10T$ and we are evaluating over a half-period $t = T/2 = 50\,\mu\text{s}$, we have $t/\tau = 0.05$. Using the Taylor expansion for small arguments:

$$e^{-t/\tau} \approx 1 - \frac{t}{\tau} + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right)^2 - \cdots$$

Therefore:

$$V_C(t) \approx V_p\left[1 - \left(1 - \frac{t}{\tau}\right)\right] = \frac{V_p\,t}{\tau}$$

**Step 7: Numerical Verification**

$$V_C(50\,\mu\text{s}) = \frac{5 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{250 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.25\,\text{V}$$

This small value (0.25 V out of 5 V) confirms the linear-ramp behavior expected from an integrator.

**Step 8: Output Waveform Description**

The output $V_C(t)$ is a **triangular waveform** with peak-to-peak amplitude:

$$\Delta V_C = \frac{V_p \cdot T}{2\tau} = \frac{5 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.25\,\text{V}_{pp}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Differentiator Response to a Square-Wave Input

**Given:** The same RC values now configured as a differentiator (output across $R$), with the same square-wave input. Here $T = 100\,\mu\text{s}$ and $\tau = RC = 1\,\mu\text{s}$ (we relabel for this derivation, taking $R = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C = 1\,\text{nF}$).

**Condition Check:** $\tau = 1\,\mu\text{s}$ and $T = 100\,\mu\text{s}$. Therefore $\tau = T/100$, satisfying $\tau \ll T$. The circuit operates as a differentiator.

**Step 1: Output Across Resistor**

$$V_{out}(t) = V_R(t) = R\,i(t) = R\,C\,\frac{dV_C(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2: Relate Output to Input**

Since $V_C + V_R = V_{in}$, and the capacitor voltage equals the input minus the output:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{in}(t) - V_C(t)$$

**Step 3: Solve the ODE for $V_C$**

For a constant input step $V_{in} = V_p$ starting at $t = 0$:

$$V_C(t) = V_p\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

**Step 4: Compute $V_R$**

$$V_R(t) = V_{in}(t) - V_C(t) = V_p - V_p\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) = V_p\,e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 5: Behaviour for $\tau \ll T$**

At $t = 0^+$, $V_R = V_p = 5\,\text{V}$ (initial spike). At $t = T/2 = 50\,\mu\text{s}$, the exponential has decayed to:

$$V_R(50\,\mu\text{s}) = 5 \times e^{-50/1} = 5 \times e^{-50} \approx 9.6 \times 10^{-22}\,\text{V} \approx 0$$

**Step 6: Output Waveform Description**

The output consists of a **positive exponential spike of amplitude $V_p$** at every rising edge of the input, and a **negative exponential spike of amplitude $-V_p$** at every falling edge. The spike decays with time constant $\tau$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theoretical Link to Differentiation:** The output $V_R = V_p e^{-t/\tau}$ for an idealized impulse-like input of unit area, $\delta(t)$, yields $V_R = RC\,\delta(t)$ — confirming that the circuit differentiates the input by extracting the rate of change concentrated at the edges.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Frequency Response of the Integrator (Bode Plot Foundation)

**Step 1: AC Steady-State Transfer Function**

Replace $s$ with $j\omega$ in the integrator transfer function:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 2: Magnitude Response**

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{\vert 1 \vert}{\sqrt{1^2 + (\omega RC)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

**Step 3: Phase Response**

$$\phi(\omega) = -\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega RC}{1}\right) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$$

**Step 4: Low-Frequency Asymptote ($\omega \to 0$)**

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert \to 1 \quad (0\,\text{dB}) \quad ; \quad \phi \to 0^\circ$$

**Step 5: High-Frequency Asymptote ($\omega \to \infty$)**

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert \approx \frac{1}{\omega RC} \quad ; \quad \phi \to -90^\circ$$

Taking $20\log_{10}(\vert H \vert)$:

$$20\log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{\omega RC}\right) = -20\log_{10}(\omega RC)$$

This is a straight line with slope **$-20$ dB/decade**.

**Step 6: Corner Frequency**

At $\omega = \omega_c = 1/RC$:

$$\vert H(j\omega_c) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707 \quad \Rightarrow \quad -3\,\text{dB}$$

The output power is half the input power at this frequency, hence the term "**$-3$ dB cut-off frequency**".

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Frequency Response of the Differentiator

**Step 1: Transfer Function**

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 2: Magnitude Response**

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

**Step 3: Low-Frequency Asymptote ($\omega \to 0$)**

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert \approx \omega RC \quad ; \quad \text{slope} = +20\,\text{dB/decade}$$

**Step 4: High-Frequency Asymptote ($\omega \to \infty$)**

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert \to 1 \quad (0\,\text{dB})$$

**Step 5: Phase Response**

$$\phi(\omega) = 90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$$

At low frequency: $\phi \to +90^\circ$. At high frequency: $\phi \to 0^\circ$.

**Step 6: Comparative Analysis Table**

| Frequency Range | Integrator Gain | Differentiator Gain |
|-----------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| $f \ll f_c$ (DC to low) | $\approx 0$ dB (passes) | $\ll 0$ dB (blocks) |
| $f = f_c$ | $-3$ dB | $-3$ dB |
| $f \gg f_c$ (high) | Strongly attenuated | $\approx 0$ dB (passes) |

---

## 3.5 Step Response Summary Table (Analytical Matrix)

| Input Waveform | Integrator Output ($V_C$) | Differentiator Output ($V_R$) |
|----------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **DC step** $V_p u(t)$ | Exponential rise: $V_p(1-e^{-t/\tau})$ | Exponential decay: $V_p e^{-t/\tau}$ |
| **Square wave** | Triangular wave (linear ramps) | Positive and negative spikes |
| **Triangular wave** | Parabolic segments (quadratic) | Square-wave-like transitions |
| **Sinusoid** $V_p \sin(\omega t)$ | $\dfrac{V_p}{\sqrt{1+(\omega RC)^2}}\sin(\omega t - \phi)$ | $\dfrac{\omega RC\,V_p}{\sqrt{1+(\omega RC)^2}}\cos(\omega t - \phi')$ |
| **Impulse** $\delta(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{RC}e^{-t/\tau}$ | $\dfrac{1}{C}\delta(t) - \dfrac{1}{RC^2}e^{-t/\tau}$ |

where $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$ and $\phi' = \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$ for the integrator and differentiator respectively.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Algorithm for Identifying Integrator vs Differentiator

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[Start Identification] --> q1{Where is output taken?}
    q1 -->|Across Capacitor| path1[Integrator Path]
    q1 -->|Across Resistor| path2[Differentiator Path]
    path1 --> c1{Compare tau and T}
    c1 -->|tau greater than 10T| label1[Good Integrator]
    c1 -->|tau less than 10T| label2[Poor Integrator LPF]
    path2 --> c2{Compare tau and T}
    c2 -->|tau less than T div 10| label3[Good Differentiator]
    c2 -->|tau greater than T div 10| label4[Poor Differentiator HPF]
    label1 --> end1[Triangular Output]
    label2 --> end2[Rounded Exponential Output]
    label3 --> end3[Spike Output]
    label4 --> end4[Distorted Transient Output]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define an RC integrating circuit. State the condition required for an RC circuit to act as a good integrator and explain its operation with a square wave input.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **RC integrating circuit** is a passive network in which the **output is taken across the capacitor** and the **time constant $\tau = RC$ is much greater than the period $T$ of the input signal** (typically $\tau \geq 10T$).

When a square wave of amplitude $V_p$ is applied, the capacitor charges and discharges through the resistor. Since $\tau$ is very large, the capacitor can charge only to a small fraction of the input voltage during each half-cycle. The current is approximately constant ($i \approx V_p/R$), so the voltage across the capacitor varies **linearly with time**, producing a **triangular waveform** at the output. Mathematically:

$$V_{out}(t) \approx \frac{1}{RC}\int V_{in}(t)\,dt$$

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is a differentiating circuit? With a neat sketch, explain its working when a square wave is applied at the input.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **differentiating circuit** is an RC network in which the **output is taken across the resistor** and the time constant $\tau = RC$ is **much smaller than the period $T$** of the input (typically $\tau \leq T/10$).

**Sketch:**

```
   Vin --R--•-- Vout
            |
            C
            |
           GND
```

**Working:** When a square wave with sharp rising and falling edges is applied, the capacitor charges almost instantaneously due to the small time constant. The output voltage across the resistor rises suddenly to the peak value $V_p$ at the rising edge and decays exponentially with time constant $\tau$. At the falling edge, a negative spike of amplitude $-V_p$ is produced. The output consists of **alternating positive and negative exponential spikes**, which approximates the derivative of the input (since differentiation of a step yields an impulse). The mathematical expression is:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_p\,e^{-t/\tau}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]
**(a)** Derive the expression for the output voltage of an RC integrator when a step input of amplitude $V_p$ is applied. Sketch the input and output waveforms. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For an RC differentiator with $R = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 1\,\mu\text{F}$, determine the output voltage when a square wave of amplitude $\pm 5\,\text{V}$ and frequency $1\,\text{kHz}$ is applied. Verify whether the circuit acts as a good differentiator. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1:** The KVL equation for the RC integrator (output across $C$) with step input $V_p u(t)$:

$$RC\,\frac{dV_C}{dt} + V_C = V_p$$

**Step 2:** The complete solution is:

$$V_C(t) = V_p + A\,e^{-t/RC}$$

**Step 3:** Applying initial condition $V_C(0) = 0$ (capacitor initially uncharged):

$$0 = V_p + A \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = -V_p$$

**Step 4:** Final expression:

$$V_C(t) = V_p\left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$$

**Step 5: Sketch:**

- Input: Step function rising from 0 to $V_p$ at $t=0$.
- Output: Exponential rise from 0, asymptotically approaching $V_p$ with time constant $\tau = RC$. At $t = \tau$, $V_C \approx 0.632 V_p$; at $t = 5\tau$, $V_C \approx 0.993 V_p$.

**Marks Distribution:** KVL setup (2), Solving ODE (2), Initial condition (1), Final expression (1), Sketch (1).

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Compute the time constant**

$$\tau = RC = (1 \times 10^3)(1 \times 10^{-6}) = 1 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 1\,\text{ms}$$

**Step 2: Compute the period of the square wave**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1 \times 10^3} = 1\,\text{ms}$$

**Step 3: Verify the differentiator condition**

$$\tau = 1\,\text{ms} = T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau \text{ is NOT } \ll T$$

The required condition is $\tau \leq T/10 = 0.1\,\text{ms}$. Since $\tau = T$, the circuit **does NOT act as a good differentiator**. It behaves as a **high-pass filter with significant phase distortion** — the output will be neither sharp spikes nor a true derivative.

**Step 4: Express the output**

For a step input at $t = 0$ with $V_{in} = +5\,\text{V}$:

$$V_{out}(t) = 5\,e^{-t/\tau} = 5\,e^{-1000t}\,\text{V}$$

At $t = T/2 = 0.5\,\text{ms}$:

$$V_{out}(0.5\,\text{ms}) = 5\,e^{-0.5} = 5 \times 0.6065 = 3.03\,\text{V}$$

This non-zero value at the next transition confirms the circuit is a **marginal high-pass filter** but not a clean differentiator.

**Conclusion:** To make it a good differentiator, either reduce $R$ or $C$ by a factor of 10 (e.g., $R = 100\,\Omega$, $C = 1\,\mu\text{F}$, giving $\tau = 0.1\,\text{ms}$).

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 - Alternative Choice]
**(a)** With the help of a circuit diagram, derive the transfer function of an RC integrator. Sketch its frequency response (Bode magnitude plot) and identify the filter type. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An RC differentiator has $R = 470\,\Omega$ and $C = 0.01\,\mu\text{F}$. Calculate the corner frequency. If a $10\,\text{kHz}$ sinusoidal signal of $2\,\text{V}_{pp}$ is applied, find the output amplitude and phase shift. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Circuit diagram** (already shown in Section 4.5 — Integrator Configuration).

**Step 2: Transfer function derivation**

Using the voltage divider rule in the s-domain:

$$H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{1/sC}{R + 1/sC} = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$

**Step 3: Frequency response**

Substituting $s = j\omega$:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 4: Magnitude and phase**

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}} \quad ; \quad \phi(\omega) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$$

**Step 5: Bode plot characteristics**

- For $f \ll f_c$: $\vert H \vert \approx 1$ (0 dB), $\phi \approx 0°$
- At $f = f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$: $\vert H \vert = 1/\sqrt{2}$ (−3 dB), $\phi = -45°$
- For $f \gg f_c$: $\vert H \vert$ rolls off at **−20 dB/decade**, $\phi \to -90°$

**Filter type:** **First-order active Low-Pass Filter (LPF)**

**Marks Distribution:** Diagram (1), Transfer function derivation (2), Magnitude and phase (2), Bode plot description (1), Filter classification (1).

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Compute the time constant**

$$\tau = RC = (470)(0.01 \times 10^{-6}) = 4.7 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s} = 4.7\,\mu\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Compute the corner frequency**

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 4.7 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 33{,}863\,\text{Hz} \approx 33.86\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step 3: Identify filter region**

Given input frequency $f = 10\,\text{kHz}$, we have $f < f_c$, so the differentiator operates in the **high-pass rising region** (gain increasing at +20 dB/decade).

**Step 4: Compute the magnitude ratio**

$$\frac{f}{f_c} = \frac{10{,}000}{33{,}863} = 0.2953$$

$$\omega RC = 2\pi f \cdot RC = 2\pi \times 10{,}000 \times 4.7 \times 10^{-6} = 0.2953$$

**Step 5: Output amplitude**

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}} = \frac{0.2953}{\sqrt{1 + 0.0872}} = \frac{0.2953}{\sqrt{1.0872}} = \frac{0.2953}{1.0427} = 0.2832$$

$$V_{out,pp} = 0.2832 \times 2\,\text{V}_{pp} = 0.566\,\text{V}_{pp}$$

**Step 6: Phase shift**

$$\phi = 90° - \tan^{-1}(0.2953) = 90° - 16.45° = +73.55°$$

The output leads the input by approximately **73.55°**.

**Marks Distribution:** Time constant (1), Corner frequency (1), Filter region identification (1), Magnitude calculation (2), Output amplitude (1), Phase shift (1).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Output Terminals:** Students often write the output across $C$ for *both* circuits. The integrator outputs are across $C$; the differentiator outputs are across $R$. **Penalty: Up to 2 marks deducted.**
>
> 2. **Ignoring the Time-Constant Condition:** Simply drawing the circuit and writing the formula is **incomplete**. The examiner expects explicit verification of $\tau \gg T$ or $\tau \ll T$ with numerical justification. **Penalty: 1–2 marks deducted.**
>
> 3. **Phase Sign Errors:** For the integrator, phase lag is **negative** (output lags input). For the differentiator, phase lead is **positive** (output leads input). Students frequently swap these signs. **Penalty: 1 mark deducted.**
>
> 4. **Using $\omega RC$ vs $f/f_c$:** Both are acceptable, but mixing them in a single derivation causes inconsistency. Choose one representation and stick with it. **Penalty: 1 mark deducted.**
>
> 5. **Forgetting Units:** Corner frequency must be in Hz, time constant in seconds, capacitance in Farads. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing or wrong units in numerical answers.
>
> 6. **Misidentifying Filter Type:** An RC integrator is a **Low-Pass Filter**; an RC differentiator is a **High-Pass Filter**. Reversing these is a common error. **Penalty: 1 mark deducted.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Same Components, Two Functions:** The RC series network acts as an **integrator** if output is across $C$ with $\tau \geq 10T$, and as a **differentiator** if output is across $R$ with $\tau \leq T/10$.
- **The Time Constant is King:** Every aspect of the circuit behaviour — output shape, filter type, corner frequency, Bode plot — is governed by $\tau = RC$.
- **Corner Frequency Formula:** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ — memorize this without error; it appears in nearly every KTU problem on this topic.
- **Integrator Equation:** $V_C(t) = V_p\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ for a step input. For $\tau \gg T$, it linearly approximates $\dfrac{1}{RC}\int V_{in}\,dt$.
- **Differentiator Equation:** $V_R(t) = V_p\,e^{-t/\tau}$ for a step input. The spike height equals $V_p$ and decays with constant $\tau$.
- **Filter Classification:** Integrator = LPF (0 dB at low freq, −20 dB/decade at high freq). Differentiator = HPF (+20 dB/decade at low freq, 0 dB at high freq).
- **Phase Sign Convention:** Integrator phase is **lagging** (negative). Differentiator phase is **leading** (positive, between 0° and +90°).
- **Square-Wave Signatures:** Integrator → **triangular wave**. Differentiator → **spike train** (alternating polarities at every edge).
- **Lab Tip:** When calibrating, always check the **−3 dB point** experimentally by sweeping the function generator frequency and confirming the output amplitude drops to $0.707 \times V_{in}$.
- **Numerical Sanity Check:** If $RC$ is in seconds and $f$ is in Hz, then $\omega RC = 2\pi f RC$ is a **dimensionless ratio**. If your calculation gives units, revisit the formula.
- **Common Component Values in KTU Labs:** $R = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $10\,\text{k}\Omega$; $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$, $1\,\mu\text{F}$. With these, $\tau$ ranges from $100\,\mu\text{s}$ to $10\,\text{ms}$.
- **Bode Plot Slope Rule:** Every first-order RC filter rolls off at **±20 dB/decade** (equivalent to **±6 dB/octave**) beyond the corner frequency.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clipping and Clamping Circuits (Transients and Transfer Characteristics)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL308 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUIT AND LOGIC DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Clipping and Clamping Circuits (Transients and Transfer Characteristics)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Clipping and Clamping Circuits

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Clipping Circuits (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

A **clipper circuit** (also called a **voltage limiter** or **amplitude selector**) is a wave-shaping electronic network that employs one or more diodes to remove (clip) portions of an input signal waveform that lie above or below a defined reference voltage level, while faithfully passing the remaining portion of the waveform to the output. The circuit is non-linear because its transfer characteristic is piecewise linear with at least one breakpoint (knee point).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition**: A clipper is a diode-based network whose transfer characteristic `Vout = f(Vin)` consists of two or more linear segments separated by a break point at the clipping reference voltage `Vref`. The output is **bounded** between an upper and/or lower threshold.

**Types of Clippers Studied in Lab:**

| # | Clipper Type | Threshold Behavior | KTU Tag |
|---|--------------|--------------------|---------|
| 1 | Positive Clipper | Clips the **positive** half of the input | Base circuit |
| 2 | Negative Clipper | Clips the **negative** half of the input | Base circuit |
| 3 | Positive Biased Clipper | Clips above `+Vref` | Extension |
| 4 | Negative Biased Clipper | Clips below `-Vref` | Extension |
| 5 | Combinational (Double-Ended) Clipper | Clips both above and below symmetric limits | Extension |

### 1.2 Formal Definition of Clamping Circuits (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

A **clamper circuit** (also called a **DC restorer** or **level shifter**) is a diode–capacitor network that shifts the entire input waveform either upward (positive clamper) or downward (negative clamper) along the voltage axis by introducing a DC offset, **without altering the peak-to-peak amplitude or shape** of the waveform. The capacitor holds the DC shift during the diode's OFF state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition**: A clamper consists of a diode in parallel with a load resistor `R`, in series with a coupling capacitor `C`. The output DC level is determined by the steady-state voltage across the capacitor, which equals the **peak input voltage** `Vm` (for ideal diode) plus any biasing voltage.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The Hedge Trimmer Analogy for Clippers**: Imagine a square hedge. A hedge trimmer blade is set at a fixed height `H`. The hedge above `H` is *cut off* (removed), and the hedge below `H` remains intact. The hedge's *shape below the cut* is preserved exactly. In a clipper, `H` corresponds to the **reference voltage** `Vref`, and the hedge corresponds to the input signal. The portion of the signal that "pokes above" the blade is removed at the output.

> [!TIP]
> **The Forklift Analogy for Clampers**: Consider a heavy wooden pallet sitting on the floor. A forklift drives underneath and *lifts the entire pallet* by exactly 1 meter — without changing its height, shape, or contents. A clamper does the same to a waveform: the *entire* waveform is translated (shifted) by a DC amount equal to its peak amplitude, preserving its shape, frequency, and peak-to-peak value. The "pallet" (AC waveform) is intact; only its *baseline* is moved.

### 1.4 Standard Physical & Circuit Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants and Standard Metrics for the Lab**:
> - **Diode forward voltage drop**: `Vf ≈ 0.7 V` (Silicon, practical) or `Vf = 0 V` (Ideal model)
> - **Cut-in voltage**: `Vγ ≈ 0.5 V` (Silicon diode at room temperature)
> - **Diode forward resistance (ON)**: `Rf ≈ 10 Ω` to `100 Ω`
> - **Diode reverse resistance (OFF)**: `Rr ≈ 10 MΩ`
> - **Standard signal frequency in lab**: `f = 1 kHz` (typical)
> - **Standard signal amplitude**: `Vm = 5 V` peak
> - **Time period**: `T = 1/f = 1 ms`
> - **Required time constant for clamper**: `τ = RC >> T/2` (typically `τ ≥ 10T`)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Positive Clipper (Series Diode Configuration)

The **positive series clipper** consists of a diode `D` in series with the load resistor `R`, with no DC bias. The input is `Vin = Vm sin(ωt)`.

**Step-by-step operating logic:**

1. **During the positive half-cycle** (`Vin > 0`): The diode is **forward biased (ON)**. Current flows through the diode, and the output follows the input minus the diode drop:
   - `Vout = Vin - Vf` (practical model)
   - `Vout = Vin` (ideal model, with `Vf = 0`)

2. **During the negative half-cycle** (`Vin < 0`): The diode is **reverse biased (OFF)**. No current flows through the series branch. The output is held at **0 V** through the load resistor:
   - `Vout = 0 V`

3. **Result**: The positive half of the waveform appears at the output (slightly attenuated by `Vf`), and the negative half is "clipped" to zero.

## 2.2 Operational Logic of a Negative Clipper (Series Diode Configuration)

The **negative series clipper** simply reverses the diode orientation.

1. **Positive half-cycle** (`Vin > 0`): Diode is **reverse biased (OFF)**. `Vout = 0 V`.
2. **Negative half-cycle** (`Vin < 0`): Diode is **forward biased (ON)**. `Vout = Vin - Vf` (or `Vin` ideal).

## 2.3 Biased Clipper Analysis (Most Important for KTU Lab)

A **biased positive clipper** adds a reference battery `Vref` in series with the diode to shift the clipping threshold above zero.

**Logic Steps:**

1. Diode becomes forward biased **only** when `Vin > Vref + Vf`.
2. For `Vin ≤ Vref + Vf`: Diode is OFF → `Vout = Vref` (clipped level).
3. For `Vin > Vref + Vf`: Diode is ON → `Vout = Vin - Vf` (linear follow).

**Breaking down the 'Why':** The reference battery `Vref` creates a DC potential barrier. The diode can only conduct when the input overcomes this barrier plus the diode's own `Vf` drop. This allows clipping at an **arbitrary** level, not just at zero.

## 2.4 Operational Logic of a Positive Clamper

A **positive clamper** consists of a **capacitor `C` in series** with the signal path, followed by a **diode `D` in parallel** with the output, and a load resistor `R` across the output.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **First positive half-cycle (capacitor charging phase)**: When `Vin` rises toward `+Vm`, the diode becomes forward biased. The diode's low forward resistance `Rf` allows the capacitor to charge rapidly. The voltage across the capacitor builds up to:
   - `Vc = Vm - Vf` (practical, ideal diode) or `Vc = Vm` (ideal model)
2. **Negative half-cycle (discharge / hold phase)**: The diode becomes reverse biased. The capacitor **cannot discharge** through the diode. It discharges only through the load resistor `R` with time constant `τ = RC`. As long as `RC >> T`, the capacitor voltage remains essentially constant at `Vm`.
3. **Steady-state output**: The output voltage is the sum of the input and the capacitor voltage:
   - `Vout(t) = Vin(t) + Vc`
   - **Peak output**: `Vout,max = +Vm + Vm = +2Vm` (positive peak)
   - **Minimum output**: `Vout,min = -Vm + Vm = 0 V`
4. **Result**: The waveform is shifted upward by `Vm`, so it lies entirely in the positive region (0 to `2Vm`).

## 2.5 Operational Logic of a Negative Clamper

Reverse the diode direction. The capacitor charges to `-Vm` during the first negative half-cycle, and shifts the waveform downward by `Vm` in steady state.

- `Vout,max = Vm - Vm = 0 V`
- `Vout,min = -Vm - Vm = -2Vm`

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Lab Exam & Viva**

| # | Formula | Description / Meaning | Units / Condition |
|---|---------|------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | `Vout = Vin` (Diode OFF) | Linear follow region | `Vin < Vthreshold` |
| 2 | `Vout = Vref` (Diode ON) | Clipped flat region | `Vin ≥ Vthreshold` |
| 3 | `Vthreshold = Vref + Vf` | Total threshold for biased clipper | Volts (V) |
| 4 | `Vc(∞) = Vm - Vf` | Capacitor steady-state voltage (practical) | Volts (V) |
| 5 | `Vc(∞) = Vm` | Capacitor steady-state voltage (ideal) | Volts (V) |
| 6 | `Vout(t) = Vin(t) + Vc` | Clamper KVL equation | Volts (V) |
| 7 | `Vout,max = 2Vm` (positive) | Maximum output of positive clamper | Volts (V) |
| 8 | `Vout,min = -2Vm` (negative) | Minimum output of negative clamper | Volts (V) |
| 9 | `τ = R · C` | Clamper discharge time constant | Seconds (s) |
| 10 | `τ = Rf · C` | Clamper charge time constant | Seconds (s) |
| 11 | `τ >> T` | Clamping condition (no sag) | Dimensionless ratio |
| 12 | `ΔV = (Vm / (R · C · f))` | Capacitor droop (tilt) per cycle | Volts (V) |
| 13 | `Slope = 1` | Transfer characteristic slope (active region) | V/V |
| 14 | `Slope = 0` | Transfer characteristic slope (clipped region) | V/V |
| 15 | `PIV ≥ Vm` | Peak Inverse Voltage rating needed for diode | Volts (V) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**: Clippers are used in **TV receivers** (to strip sync pulses), in **radar systems** (to limit noise spikes), in **AM/FM demodulators**, and in **over-voltage protection** circuits. Clampers are used in **oscilloscope input stages** (AC coupling with DC restoration), in **TV video amplifiers** (DC restoration after AC coupling), in **pulse circuits** (baseline stabilization), and in **sample-and-hold** amplifiers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Transfer Characteristic for an Ideal Positive Series Clipper

**Given:** A positive series clipper with an ideal diode (`Vf = 0`) in series with load `R`. Input: `Vin = Vm sin(ωt)`.

**Step 1 — Identify the diode state conditions.**

The diode is forward biased when the anode voltage is greater than the cathode voltage. With no bias source, the cathode is effectively at the output node, and the anode is at the input. Therefore:

$$\text{Diode is ON} \iff V_{in} > 0$$

$$\text{Diode is OFF} \iff V_{in} \leq 0$$

**Step 2 — Write the output equation for each region.**

*Region 1 (Diode ON, Vin > 0):* Current flows. With ideal diode, there is no voltage drop, so:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} - V_{f} = V_{in} - 0 = V_{in}$$

*Region 2 (Diode OFF, Vin ≤ 0):* No current flows. The output is pulled to ground through the load resistor `R`:

$$V_{out} = 0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Construct the transfer characteristic (Vin vs Vout).**

The transfer characteristic is piecewise linear:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{out}(V_{in}) &= \begin{cases} V_{in}, & \text{if } V_{in} > 0 \\ 0, & \text{if } V_{in} \leq 0 \end{cases}
\end{aligned}$$

The slope in the active region is `dVout/dVin = 1`, and in the clipped region is `0`. The break-point (knee) occurs at `Vin = 0`.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Clamper Capacitor Voltage in Steady State

**Given:** A positive clamper with `C` in series, diode `D` (ideal) in parallel with output, load `R`. Input: `Vin = Vm sin(ωt)`.

**Step 1 — Apply KVL around the loop during the first positive half-cycle.**

The loop consists of: input source, capacitor, diode (ON), and back to source. Since the diode is ideal and ON, the voltage across the diode is zero:

$$V_{in} - V_{c} - V_{D} = 0$$

$$V_{in} - V_{c} - 0 = 0$$

$$V_{c} = V_{in}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate at the input peak.**

At `t = T/4`, the input reaches its positive peak `Vm`:

$$V_{c}\left(\frac{T}{4}\right) = V_{m}$$

**Step 3 — Analyze the subsequent half-cycle (diode OFF phase).**

When the diode is OFF, no current flows through the diode. The capacitor can only discharge through `R`. The KVL is now:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} - V_{c}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the steady-state condition.**

If `RC >> T`, the capacitor holds its charge. In steady state, the capacitor voltage equals the peak input:

$$V_{c,\infty} = V_{m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the steady-state output extrema.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{out,max} &= V_{in,max} - V_{c,\infty} = V_{m} - (-V_{m}) = 2V_{m} \\
V_{out,min} &= V_{in,min} - V_{c,\infty} = -V_{m} - (-V_{m}) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

(Wait — careful with sign convention. In clamper analysis, `Vout = Vin + Vc` when `Vc` is defined with polarity opposing `Vin` during conduction. The standard textbook form gives `Vout,max = +Vm + Vm = +2Vm` for the positive clamper.)

**Step 6 — Tilt (sag) calculation per cycle.**

The droop in capacitor voltage per cycle (assuming small droop) is:

$$\Delta V_{c} = \frac{V_{m}}{R \cdot C \cdot f}$$

This is the maximum deviation from the ideal flat DC level — a critical design parameter to verify in the lab oscilloscope trace.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Biased Clipper Threshold

**Given:** A positive biased clipper with reference battery `Vref` (positive polarity) opposing the diode's forward conduction. Input: `Vin`.

**Step 1 — Identify the diode ON condition.**

The diode becomes forward biased when the input voltage overcomes both the reference and the diode's cut-in voltage:

$$V_{in} > V_{ref} + V_{f}$$

**Step 2 — Define the clipping threshold.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{threshold} &= V_{ref} + V_{f} \\
&\approx V_{ref} \quad (\text{ideal diode, } V_{f}=0)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Construct the piecewise output equation.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{out}(V_{in}) &= \begin{cases} V_{in} - V_{f}, & \text{if } V_{in} > V_{ref} + V_{f} \\ V_{ref}, & \text{if } V_{in} \leq V_{ref} + V_{f} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Special case: Vref = 0.**

This reduces to the unbiased positive clipper from §3.1.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Numerical)

**Problem:** A positive biased clipper uses `Vref = 3 V` and a silicon diode. The input is `Vin(t) = 10 sin(314t) V`. Sketch the output waveform and write the transfer characteristic.

**Step 1 — Identify the threshold.**

$$V_{threshold} = V_{ref} + V_{f} = 3 + 0.7 = 3.7 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Determine the output values at key points.**

- At `Vin = +10 V` (peak): `Vout = 10 - 0.7 = 9.3 V`
- At `Vin = +3.7 V` (threshold): `Vout = 3.7 - 0.7 = 3.0 V` (transition begins)
- At `Vin = 0 V`: `Vout = 3.0 V` (clipped flat)
- At `Vin = -10 V` (negative peak): `Vout = 3.0 V` (clipped flat)

**Step 3 — Transfer characteristic.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{out}(V_{in}) &= \begin{cases} V_{in} - 0.7, & V_{in} > 3.7 \text{ V} \\ 3.0, & V_{in} \leq 3.7 \text{ V} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Verify peak-to-peak output.**

$$V_{out,pp} = 9.3 - 3.0 = 6.3 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Sketch:** The output is a sine wave with negative half-cycle and lower positive portion flattened at 3.0 V, peaking at 9.3 V.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Clipper Circuit

The following block diagram maps the **functional signal flow** within a generic biased clipper, showing the decision node (diode), the threshold comparator logic, and the two output paths.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    srcA[Vin Source: Sine Wave Generator] --> capA[Input Coupling Node]
    capA --> node1{Diode Decision Node}
    node1 --> onPathA[ON Branch: Vin greater than Vref plus Vf]
    node1 --> offPathB[OFF Branch: Vin less than or equal to Vref plus Vf]
    onPathA --> linearA[Linear Follow Block: Vout equals Vin minus Vf]
    offPathB --> clampA[Clamp Block: Vout equals Vref Constant]
    linearA --> joinA[Output Summation Junction]
    clampA --> joinA
    joinA --> loadA[Load Resistor RL]
    loadA --> outA[Vout Terminal: Observed on CRO]
    biasA[Bias Battery Vref] --> node1
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Clamper Circuit

This block diagram shows the charging path (during diode conduction) and the discharging / hold path (during diode reverse bias) of a positive clamper.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    srcB[Vin Source: Sine Wave Generator] --> capB[Series Capacitor C]
    capB --> node2{Diode State Decision}
    node2 --> chgPath[Charging Path: Diode ON, Rf Low]
    node2 --> holdPath[Holding Path: Diode OFF, No Conduction]
    chgPath --> chgBlock[Capacitor Charges to Vm via Rf times C]
    holdBlock[Capacitor Holds Charge: Vc approximately Vm] --> holdPath
    chgBlock --> sumB[KVL Summation: Vout equals Vin plus Vc]
    holdPath --> sumB
    sumB --> loadB[Load Resistor R to Ground]
    loadB --> outB[Vout Terminal: DC Shifted Waveform on CRO]
```

## 4.3 Transfer Characteristic Map (Conceptual Block View)

The transfer characteristic `Vout = f(Vin)` for a **positive biased clipper** has two distinct linear regions. The following block view shows the active and clipped segments.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisA[Vin Axis: Input Voltage] --> segA[Active Segment: Slope 1, Vout equals Vin minus Vf]
    axisA --> segB[Clipped Segment: Slope 0, Vout equals Vref Constant]
    kneeA[Knee Point at Vin equals Vref plus Vf] --> segA
    kneeA --> segB
    segA --> outMap[Output Plot: Rising Line Follows Input]
    segB --> outMap
    outMap --> labelA[Region 1: Linear Follow Above Threshold]
    outMap --> labelB[Region 2: Flat Clipped Region Below Threshold]
```

## 4.4 Transient Response Block View for Clamper Startup

The first few cycles of a clamper's output are not yet at steady state. The capacitor needs multiple cycles to reach its final voltage. This block diagram shows the **transient envelope** as the circuit approaches steady state.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    t0[Cycle 1: Capacitor Charges to Partial Vc] --> t1[Cycle 2: Vc Approaches Vm]
    t1 --> t2[Cycle 3 to 5: Vc nearly Vm]
    t2 --> tss[Steady State: Vc equals Vm, Vout swings from 0 to 2Vm]
    t0 --> obsA[Oscilloscope Trace: Asymmetric with Tilted Peaks]
    t1 --> obsB[Oscilloscope Trace: Less Asymmetry, Reduced Tilt]
    t2 --> obsC[Oscilloscope Trace: Nearly Flat Top, Small Droop]
    tss --> obsD[Oscilloscope Trace: Ideal Clamped Waveform]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Lab Tip for Mermaid Block Diagrams**: When drawing actual schematics is not possible, the block-level functional architecture conveys the *decision logic* of the diode (ON/OFF) and the *signal path* of the capacitor. Students should annotate their lab record with both the physical circuit diagram (drawn freehand) and one such functional block diagram.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**Q: Define a clipper circuit. With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a positive clipper and sketch its transfer characteristic.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A clipper is a diode-based wave-shaping circuit that limits the amplitude of an input signal by removing portions that exceed a reference voltage level.

In a **positive series clipper**, a diode is connected in series with the load. During the positive half-cycle, the diode is forward biased (ON) and the output follows the input: `Vout = Vin - Vf`. During the negative half-cycle, the diode is reverse biased (OFF) and the output is held at 0 V.

**Transfer characteristic:** A piecewise linear graph with slope 1 for `Vin > 0` and slope 0 (flat at zero) for `Vin ≤ 0`. The break point is at `Vin = 0`. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for circuit + operation, 1 for transfer characteristic)**

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: What is a clamping circuit? State the necessary condition on the time constant `RC` for proper clamping and justify it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A clamping circuit is a diode–capacitor network that shifts the DC level of a waveform without changing its peak-to-peak amplitude.

**Condition:** `τ = RC >> T`, where `T` is the time period of the input signal. Typically, `RC ≥ 10T`.

**Justification:** During the diode-OFF phase, the capacitor discharges through the load resistor `R`. If `RC` is not sufficiently larger than `T`, the capacitor will lose a significant fraction of its charge between cycles, causing the output waveform to droop (tilt) and fail to maintain a stable DC level. A large `RC` ensures the discharge is negligible per cycle, producing a clean clamped waveform. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for condition, 1 for justification)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule**: Part B questions in lab courses carry 14 marks and follow internal choice. Each sub-part carries 7 marks. The model solution below follows the standard KTU valuation key pattern: **3 marks for circuit/derivation, 2 marks for calculation, 2 marks for waveform/sketch**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**For a positive biased clipper circuit with `Vref = 4 V` and silicon diode (`Vf = 0.7 V`), with input `Vin(t) = 12 sin(100πt) V` and load `RL = 10 kΩ`:**

**(a) Draw the circuit diagram and derive the transfer characteristic equation. Sketch the output waveform. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Calculate the threshold voltage, the output peak, and the minimum output. Also compute the peak inverse voltage (PIV) the diode must withstand. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1 — Circuit:** Diode `D` in series with load `RL`, with a DC battery `Vref = 4 V` opposing the diode's forward conduction (anode to input, cathode to Vref, then to output).

**Step 2 — Derive the threshold:**

$$V_{threshold} = V_{ref} + V_f = 4 + 0.7 = 4.7 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Piecewise output equation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{out}(V_{in}) &= \begin{cases} V_{in} - 0.7, & V_{in} > 4.7 \text{ V} \\ 4.0, & V_{in} \leq 4.7 \text{ V} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Sketch description:** The output is a sine wave that follows the input above 4.7 V, and is flattened at 4.0 V for the rest of the cycle (entire negative half-cycle and lower portion of positive half-cycle).

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1 — Threshold (already):** `Vthreshold = 4.7 V`

**Step 2 — Output peak:**

$$V_{out,peak} = V_{in,peak} - V_f = 12 - 0.7 = 11.3 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Minimum output:**

$$V_{out,min} = V_{ref} = 4.0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV):**

The maximum reverse voltage across the diode occurs when the input is at its negative peak and the diode is OFF:

$$PIV = \vert V_{in,min} \vert + V_{ref} = 12 + 4 = 16 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Selection of diode:** A diode with `PIV ≥ 16 V` must be used (e.g., 1N4007 with 1000 V PIV is suitable).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**For a positive clamper circuit with `C = 1 μF`, `R = 100 kΩ`, ideal diode, and input `Vin(t) = 10 sin(2000πt) V`:**

**(a) Draw the circuit, explain the operation during each half-cycle, and derive the steady-state capacitor voltage. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Calculate the time period, verify the clamping condition, and determine the output voltage waveform extrema and the tilt per cycle. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1 — Circuit:** Capacitor `C` in series with signal path, diode `D` (anode to ground, cathode to output) in parallel, and load `R` from output to ground.

**Step 2 — Positive half-cycle operation:** When `Vin` rises, the diode becomes forward biased. With ideal diode, the capacitor charges to the peak input voltage through zero resistance: `Vc = Vm = 10 V`. The polarity of Vc is such that the left plate (input side) is positive.

**Step 3 — Negative half-cycle operation:** When `Vin` goes negative, the diode becomes reverse biased. No current flows through the diode branch. The capacitor can only discharge through `R`.

**Step 4 — Steady-state:** If `RC >> T`, the capacitor holds its charge. By KVL: `Vout = Vin + Vc`. Since `Vc = Vm = 10 V` (with polarity adding to Vin), the waveform is shifted upward by 10 V.

**Step 5 — Derivation:**

$$V_{c,\infty} = V_m = 10 \text{ V}$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1 — Time period:**

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega} = \frac{2\pi}{2000\pi} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Time constant:**

$$\tau = R \cdot C = 100 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-6} = 0.1 \text{ s} = 100 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Verify clamping condition:**

$$\frac{\tau}{T} = \frac{100 \text{ ms}}{1 \text{ ms}} = 100 \gg 1 \quad \checkmark$$

The condition is satisfied; the clamper will operate correctly with negligible droop.

**Step 4 — Output extrema:**

$$V_{out,max} = +V_m + V_m = +2V_m = +20 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{out,min} = -V_m + V_m = 0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Tilt per cycle:**

$$\Delta V_c = \frac{V_m}{R \cdot C \cdot f} = \frac{10}{100 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 1000} = 0.1 \text{ V per cycle}$$

This is a 1% tilt, which is visually negligible on the oscilloscope.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> - **Mark Loss Point 1**: Students often write the transfer characteristic as a single equation `Vout = Vin` for the entire range, forgetting the piecewise nature. Always show the break point.
> - **Mark Loss Point 2**: Confusing **series** and **shunt** clippers. In series, the diode is in the signal path; in shunt (parallel), the diode is across the load. The current direction and clipping behavior reverse.
> - **Mark Loss Point 3**: Forgetting to add `Vf` when the question specifies a silicon diode. Always check whether the question asks for *ideal* or *practical* diode model.
> - **Mark Loss Point 4**: In clamper problems, students frequently write `Vout = Vin - Vc` instead of `Vout = Vin + Vc`. The sign depends on the **polarity convention** of Vc. The KTU convention is `Vout = Vin + Vc` where Vc is the voltage drop in the direction of current flow during the charging half-cycle.
> - **Mark Loss Point 5**: Not specifying the **time constant condition** `RC >> T` in clamper answers. This is a frequent viva question and carries direct marks.
> - **Mark Loss Point 6**: Forgetting to compute the **PIV** rating of the diode in biased clipper problems. This is often asked in the 7-mark sub-part.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Lab Exam & Viva**

- **Clipper**: A diode circuit that **removes** portions of a signal. Two types — **series** (diode in signal path) and **shunt** (diode across load).
- **Clamper**: A diode–capacitor circuit that **shifts** the DC level of a signal. Two types — **positive** (shifts up) and **negative** (shifts down).
- **Positive clipper**: Removes positive half; **negative clipper** removes negative half.
- **Biased clipper**: Clipping threshold = `Vref + Vf` (practical) or `Vref` (ideal).
- **Clamper threshold condition**: `RC >> T` (usually `RC ≥ 10T`).
- **Steady-state capacitor voltage** in ideal clamper: `Vc = Vm` (positive clamper) or `Vc = -Vm` (negative clamper).
- **Positive clamper output**: `Vout,max = +2Vm`, `Vout,min = 0 V`.
- **Negative clamper output**: `Vout,max = 0 V`, `Vout,min = -2Vm`.
- **Transfer characteristic slope**: 1 (V/V) in active region, 0 (V/V) in clipped region.
- **Tilt (droop)** per cycle: `ΔVc = Vm / (RCf)`. Smaller for larger `RC` and higher `f`.
- **PIV** for biased clipper diode: `PIV = \vert Vin,min \vert + Vref`.
- **Practical diode `Vf`**: 0.7 V (Silicon) — include this whenever the question says "silicon diode" or "practical diode".
- **Oscilloscope setup in lab**: Use **AC coupling** to view the AC waveform and **DC coupling** to verify the shifted DC level after clamping.
- **Function generator settings**: 1 kHz sine wave, 5–10 Vpp is standard for lab experiments.
- **Viva question**: "What happens if the diode is reversed in a positive clamper?" → It becomes a negative clamper.
- **Viva question**: "What is the role of the load resistor in a clamper?" → It provides the discharge path for the capacitor during the diode-OFF phase, and its value (with C) sets the time constant.
- **Viva question**: "Why can't we use a clamper at very high frequencies?" → At high frequencies, the diode's reverse recovery time and the capacitor's parasitic inductance degrade the clamping action.
- **Common lab components**: 1N4007 (rectifier diode), 1 μF / 10 μF electrolytic capacitor, 10 kΩ resistor, breadboard, function generator, CRO (or DSO).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RC Coupled CE Amplifier - Frequency Response Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL308 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUIT AND LOGIC DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** RC Coupled CE Amplifier - Frequency Response Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **RC Coupled Common Emitter (CE) Amplifier** is a multi-stage, small-signal voltage amplifier in which the output of one common-emitter BJT stage is coupled to the input of the next stage through a **Resistor-Capacitor (RC) network**. The coupling capacitor blocks the DC bias of the first stage from interfering with the DC bias of the next, while the resistor sets the input impedance of the following stage.

The **Frequency Response** of this amplifier is a plot of the voltage gain ($A_v$) versus the input signal frequency ($f$), typically expressed on a **semi-logarithmic scale** (frequency on log axis, gain in dB on linear axis). It exhibits three distinct regions: a **low-frequency region**, a **mid-frequency (flat-band) region**, and a **high-frequency region**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The frequency response characterizes the variation of the magnitude of the amplifier gain as a function of the operating frequency. It is bounded by the **lower cutoff frequency ($f_L$)** and the **upper cutoff frequency ($f_H$)**, and the span between them is the **bandwidth ($BW$)**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **stadium megaphone** system where multiple people stand in a line, each amplifying the voice of the person before them. Now imagine the following real-world challenges:

- **Very low frequencies (slow rumbles):** The coupling capacitor between the speakers acts like a **high-pass filter that blocks slow DC drift** — like a turnstile that only lets through people moving above a certain speed. So very low frequencies get attenuated.
- **Mid frequencies (normal speech):** The turnstile spins freely, and each person amplifies the message cleanly. The **gain is at its maximum and flat** — this is the **mid-band region**.
- **Very high frequencies (screaming/shrill tones):** The internal wiring of the speakers (wires, transistor capacitances) acts like a **low-pass filter** — like a narrow pipe that chokes off water flow when the pressure becomes too fast. So very high frequencies also get attenuated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why RC Coupling?** The "RC" refers to the **resistor (R)** that carries the amplified signal and the **capacitor (C)** that blocks DC while passing AC. This combination gives excellent impedance matching between stages, making RC coupling the **most widely used coupling method in audio amplifiers** (e.g., public address systems, radio receivers, televisions, tape recorders).

---

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

- **Voltage Gain ($A_v$):** Unitless ratio, typically **20 to 200** for a single stage.
- **Gain in dB:** $A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\vert A_v \vert\right)$. Standard mid-band gain: **26 dB to 46 dB** per stage.
- **Lower Cutoff Frequency ($f_L$):** Frequency at which gain falls to **0.707 $A_{v(\text{mid})}$** (or **−3 dB point**). Typical range: **20 Hz to 200 Hz**.
- **Upper Cutoff Frequency ($f_H$):** Frequency at which gain again falls to **−3 dB** of mid-band. Typical range: **20 kHz to 500 kHz**.
- **Bandwidth ($BW$):** $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ (since $f_H \gg f_L$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the PCBRL308 lab, the standard experiment uses a **BC547 NPN BJT** (or equivalent) with $V_{CC} = \mathbf{+12\text{ V}}$, and the bandwidth is measured using a function generator and CRO. Students are expected to identify the **3 dB points** directly from the observed waveform.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle of RC Coupled CE Amplifier

The RC coupled CE amplifier operates on the principle of **biasing stability and AC signal amplification through emitter biasing**, with cascaded stages connected via RC coupling networks.

**Step-by-Step Operating Logic:**

1. **DC Biasing:** The BJT is biased in the **active region** using a **voltage divider bias** ($R_1, R_2$) and an **emitter resistor** ($R_E$) bypassed by $C_E$. This ensures the Q-point is stabilized against temperature variations.
2. **Input Coupling:** The AC input signal $V_{in}$ is applied through coupling capacitor $C_{in}$, which blocks any DC offset from the source.
3. **Amplification:** The small AC signal modulates the base-emitter junction. The amplified, **phase-inverted (180°)** output appears at the collector.
4. **Output Coupling:** Capacitor $C_{out}$ blocks the DC collector voltage and passes only the AC component to the load (or to the next stage).
5. **Inter-stage Coupling:** Between two stages, $C_C$ (coupling capacitor) connects the collector of stage 1 to the base of stage 2. $R_C$ of stage 1 acts as the collector load; this resistor, together with $C_C$ and the input resistance of the next stage, forms a **high-pass filter** that determines the lower cutoff frequency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three cutoff-determining capacitors?**
> - $C_{in}$: Source to base — contributes to $f_L$.
> - $C_E$: Bypass capacitor across $R_E$ — major contributor to $f_L$ (because $R_E$ is typically large).
> - $C_{out}$ (or $C_C$): Collector to load — contributes to $f_L$.

### 2.2 Three Regions of Frequency Response

**Region 1 — Low-Frequency Region ($f < f_L$):**
- The reactance of the coupling and bypass capacitors becomes **significant** (large).
- The voltage gain **rolls off at +20 dB/decade** as frequency decreases.
- This is governed by the high-pass RC networks at the input, output, and emitter.

**Region 2 — Mid-Frequency Region ($f_L \le f \le f_H$):**
- All coupling and bypass capacitors act as **AC short circuits** ($X_C \to 0$).
- All internal transistor capacitances (like $C_{be}$, $C_{bc}$) act as **AC open circuits**.
- Gain is **constant and maximum**: $A_v = A_{v(\text{mid})} = -g_m R_{ac}$, where $R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L \parallel r_o$.

**Region 3 — High-Frequency Region ($f > f_H$):**
- The reactance of **internal transistor capacitances** ($C_{be}$, $C_{bc}$, or equivalently the **Miller capacitance $C_M$**) becomes significant.
- The voltage gain **rolls off at −20 dB/decade** as frequency increases.
- The transistor's high-frequency performance is limited by its **transition frequency $f_T$**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** |
|------------|--------------|-------------|-----------|
| $A_v$ | Voltage gain (mid-band) | $A_v = -g_m R_{ac}$ | dimensionless |
| $g_m$ | Transconductance | $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T} = \dfrac{\vert I_C \vert}{26\text{ mV}}$ | Siemens (S) |
| $R_{ac}$ | AC load resistance | $R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| $A_v$(dB) | Gain in decibels | $A_v$(dB) $= 20 \log_{10}\vert A_v \vert$ | decibels (dB) |
| $f_L$ | Lower cutoff frequency | $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_{in}} + \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L) C_{out}} + \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_E C_E}$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| $f_H$ | Upper cutoff frequency | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{M}}$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| $C_M$ | Miller capacitance | $C_M = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ | Farads (F) |
| $BW$ | Bandwidth | $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| $f_T$ | Transition frequency | $f_T = g_m / 2\pi (C_{be} + C_{bc})$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| $r_\pi$ | Base-emitter dynamic resistance | $r_\pi = \beta / g_m = \beta V_T / \vert I_C \vert$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| $R_{in}$ | Input resistance of stage | $R_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| $V_T$ | Thermal voltage | $V_T \approx 26\text{ mV}$ at **300 K** | Volts (V) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Note:** The **3 dB frequencies** are defined as the points where the gain drops to **0.7071** of the mid-band value. The total low-frequency cutoff when multiple capacitors contribute is **not** the simple sum — for non-interacting sections, the dominant pole is used. For KTU problems, calculate each $f_L$ contribution and add them if they are widely separated (Bode approximation).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio amplifiers** (PA systems, home theaters) — RC coupling provides flat gain across the **20 Hz – 20 kHz** audio band.
- **Radio/TV IF amplifiers** — narrow-band RC coupled stages provide **selectivity**.
- **Oscilloscope vertical amplifiers** — multi-stage RC coupled design ensures wide bandwidth.
- **Public address and communication systems** — cascaded RC stages boost weak signals to drive loudspeakers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not transformer coupling?** Transformers are bulky, expensive, and introduce distortion at low frequencies. RC coupling is **cheap, lightweight, and provides excellent frequency response**, which is why it dominates commercial audio electronics.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Mid-Band Voltage Gain

The mid-band small-signal hybrid-π model of a CE amplifier is used.

**Step 1:** Identify the AC small-signal parameters.

The collector current is:

$$
I_C = g_m V_{be}
$$

where $g_m$ is the transconductance of the transistor, and $V_{be}$ is the small-signal base-emitter voltage.

**Step 2:** Identify the AC load seen at the collector.

In the mid-band region, all capacitors are short circuits. The collector sees $R_C$ in parallel with the load $R_L$ (and the next stage's input if cascaded):

$$
R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L
$$

**Step 3:** Write the output voltage.

The output voltage at the collector (taken with respect to the AC ground at the emitter) is:

$$
V_{out} = -I_C \cdot R_{ac} = -g_m V_{be} \cdot R_{ac}
$$

**Step 4:** Identify the input voltage.

For a CE amplifier, the input voltage $V_{in}$ appears directly across $V_{be}$ (assuming the source resistance is small compared to $r_\pi$):

$$
V_{in} = V_{be}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the voltage gain.

$$
A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{-g_m V_{be} R_{ac}}{V_{be}} = -g_m R_{ac}
$$

**Result:**

$$
A_v = -g_m R_{ac}
$$

The negative sign indicates a **180° phase inversion** between input and output — a hallmark of the CE configuration.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Lower Cutoff Frequency

The lower cutoff frequency arises because the coupling and bypass capacitors introduce high-pass RC networks. We analyze each contribution separately.

**Contribution 1: Input Coupling Capacitor $C_{in}$**

The Thevenin resistance seen by $C_{in}$ is the source resistance $R_S$ in series with the input resistance of the amplifier $R_{in}$:

$$
R_{in}' = R_S + R_{in}
$$

The cutoff frequency contributed by $C_{in}$:

$$
f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{in}' C_{in}}
$$

**Step-by-step logic:** As frequency decreases, $X_{Cin} = 1/(2\pi f C_{in})$ increases. When $X_{Cin}$ becomes comparable to $R_{in}'$, the input signal is dropped across the capacitor, reducing the voltage reaching the base. The −3 dB point is where $X_{Cin} = R_{in}'$.

**Contribution 2: Output Coupling Capacitor $C_{out}$**

The Thevenin resistance seen by $C_{out}$ is $R_C$ in series with $R_L$:

$$
R_{out}' = R_C + R_L
$$

The cutoff frequency contributed:

$$
f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L) C_{out}}
$$

**Contribution 3: Emitter Bypass Capacitor $C_E$**

The resistance seen by $C_E$ is **not simply $R_E$** — it is $R_E$ reflected through the transistor action. In the small-signal model, the emitter node sees $R_E$ in parallel with the resistance $r_e + R_S/(\beta+1)$ looking back into the emitter. The dominant term is:

$$
R_E' = R_E \parallel \left(r_e + \frac{R_S \parallel R_B}{\beta+1}\right)
$$

For most practical cases, $R_E \gg r_e$, so:

$$
R_E' \approx R_E
$$

The cutoff frequency contributed:

$$
f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_E' C_E}
$$

**Step-by-step logic:** When $X_{CE}$ is small, $R_E$ is bypassed and the gain is maximum. When frequency is low, $X_{CE}$ is large, $R_E$ is no longer bypassed, and the gain drops because of **local negative feedback**.

**Total Lower Cutoff Frequency (Bode Approximation):**

$$
f_L \approx f_{L1} + f_{L2} + f_{L3}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_{in}} + \frac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L) C_{out}} + \frac{1}{2\pi R_E C_E}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Upper Cutoff Frequency (Miller Effect)

The upper cutoff frequency is governed by the **internal parasitic capacitances** of the BJT, primarily $C_{be}$ (base-emitter) and $C_{bc}$ (base-collector).

**Step 1:** The collector-base capacitance $C_{bc}$ is multiplied by the Miller effect because the voltage gain from base to collector is $-A_v$. The Miller capacitance is:

$$
C_M = C_{bc}(1 + A_v)
$$

**Step-by-step logic:** The capacitor $C_{bc}$ connects the input node (base) to the output node (collector). The current through it depends on the voltage difference $V_b - V_c = V_{be} - V_{out}$. Since $V_{out} = -A_v V_{be}$, the differential voltage is $V_{be}(1 + A_v)$, which is much larger — hence the amplification of effective capacitance at the input.

**Step 2:** The total input capacitance is the sum of $C_{be}$ and $C_M$:

$$
C_{in(\text{total})} = C_{be} + C_M = C_{be} + C_{bc}(1 + A_v)
$$

**Step 3:** The Thevenin resistance seen at the input is $R_S \parallel R_{in}$. Let this be $R_{eq}$:

$$
R_{eq} = R_S \parallel (R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi)
$$

**Step 4:** The upper cutoff frequency is:

$$
\boxed{f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} [C_{be} + C_{bc}(1 + A_v)]}}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Bandwidth

The bandwidth is the difference between the upper and lower cutoff frequencies:

$$
BW = f_H - f_L
$$

For practical audio amplifiers, $f_H \gg f_L$, so:

$$
BW \approx f_H
$$

**Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP):**

The product of mid-band gain and bandwidth is a constant for a given transistor:

$$
\text{GBP} = A_v \cdot BW \approx \frac{g_m}{2\pi C_M}
$$

This is a critical figure of merit — a transistor with high GBP can be used in high-gain, wide-bandwidth applications.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:** BC547 BJT with $I_C = 2\text{ mA}$, $R_C = 4.7\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $C_{in} = C_{out} = 10\text{ }\mu\text{F}$, $C_E = 100\text{ }\mu\text{F}$, $R_E = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_S = 600\text{ }\Omega$, $C_{be} = 20\text{ pF}$, $C_{bc} = 5\text{ pF}$, $\beta = 200$.

**Step 1:** Calculate $g_m$.

$$
g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0769\text{ S} = 76.9\text{ mS}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate mid-band gain.

$$
R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{4.7 \times 10}{14.7} = 3.197\text{ k}\Omega
$$

$$
A_v = -g_m R_{ac} = -0.0769 \times 3197 = -245.9
$$

In dB:

$$
A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(245.9) \approx 47.8\text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate lower cutoff contributions.

$r_\pi = \beta / g_m = 200 / 0.0769 = 2.6\text{ k}\Omega$. With $R_1 = 47\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_{in} = 47 \parallel 10 \parallel 2.6 \approx 2.0\text{ k}\Omega$.

$$
f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi (600 + 2000) \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2600 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 6.12\text{ Hz}
$$

$$
f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi (4700 + 10000) \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 14700 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 1.08\text{ Hz}
$$

$$
f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 0.1} \approx 1.59\text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4:** Total $f_L$ (Bode sum).

$$
f_L = 6.12 + 1.08 + 1.59 = 8.79\text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 5:** Calculate upper cutoff.

$A_v = 245.9$, $C_M = 5 \times 10^{-12} \times (1 + 245.9) = 1.2345\text{ nF}$.

$R_{eq} = 600 \parallel 2000 = 461.5\text{ }\Omega$.

$$
f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 461.5 \times (20 \times 10^{-12} + 1.2345 \times 10^{-9})} \approx 275.7\text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 6:** Bandwidth.

$$
BW = f_H - f_L = 275700 - 8.79 \approx 275.7\text{ kHz}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Two-Stage RC Coupled CE Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[AC Signal Source] -->|Vin| cinNode[C_in Coupling]
    cinNode --> biasA[R1 R2 Voltage Divider Bias]
    biasA --> bjtA[Q1 BC547 CE Stage 1]
    bjtA -->|180 deg phase shift| ccNode[C_c Coupling Capacitor]
    ccNode --> biasB[R3 R4 Voltage Divider Bias Stage 2]
    biasB --> bjtB[Q2 BC547 CE Stage 2]
    bjtB --> coutNode[C_out Coupling]
    coutNode --> loadNode[R_L Load Speaker]
    bjtA --> reA[R_E1 with C_E1 bypass]
    bjtB --> reB[R_E2 with C_E2 bypass]
    vccNode[Vcc +12V DC Supply] --> bjtA
    vccNode --> bjtB
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Frequency Response Regions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    fLow[Low Frequency f less than f_L] --> capA[Coupling Capacitors Block AC]
    fMid[Mid Frequency f_L to f_H] --> capB[All Caps Short Circuits]
    fHigh[High Frequency f greater than f_H] --> capC[Parasitic Capacitors Shunt AC]
    capA --> rollA[Gain Rolls Off at plus 20 dB per decade]
    capB --> flat[Gain Flat at A_v max in dB]
    capC --> rollB[Gain Rolls Off at minus 20 dB per decade]
    flat --> fL[P1 Lower Cutoff at minus 3 dB]
    flat --> fH[P2 Upper Cutoff at minus 3 dB]
    fL --> bw[Bandwidth BW = f_H minus f_L]
    fH --> bw
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Signal Flow and Frequency Determining Components

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    input[Input Source] --> cin[C_in]
    cin --> bjt[CE Transistor]
    bjt --> ce[C_E bypass]
    bjt --> cout[C_out]
    cout --> output[Output Load]
    cin -.->|determines| fL1[f_L1 contribution]
    ce -.->|determines| fL2[f_L2 contribution]
    cout -.->|determines| fL3[f_L3 contribution]
    bjt -.->|Miller effect| fH[f_H upper cutoff]
    fL1 --> fLtotal[Total f_L]
    fL2 --> fLtotal
    fL3 --> fLtotal
    fLtotal --> bwcalc[Bandwidth Calculation]
    fH --> bwcalc
    bwcalc --> resp[Frequency Response Plot]
```

### 4.4 Frequency Response Plot Description (Bode Plot)

The frequency response is plotted with **frequency on the x-axis (logarithmic scale, Hz)** and **gain in dB on the y-axis (linear scale)**. The shape resembles a **band-pass filter** with three distinct regions:

- **Low-frequency roll-off:** Straight line with slope **+20 dB/decade** (gain increases as frequency increases, then plateaus).
- **Mid-band flat region:** Horizontal line at the mid-band gain (e.g., **47.8 dB** in the worked example).
- **High-frequency roll-off:** Straight line with slope **−20 dB/decade** (gain decreases as frequency increases).
- The two corners at $f_L$ and $f_H$ are the **−3 dB points** where the gain is $0.707 A_{v(\text{mid})}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Note:** When performing the experiment, students should tabulate readings of input voltage ($V_i$), output voltage ($V_o$), and frequency ($f$) at multiple points — typically 10 points in each decade. Then compute gain in dB and plot the response on **semi-log graph paper**. The bandwidth is read directly as the horizontal distance between the two −3 dB points.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Define the term **bandwidth** of an RC coupled amplifier. How is it related to the cutoff frequencies?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **bandwidth (BW)** of an RC coupled amplifier is defined as the range of frequencies over which the amplifier provides useful amplification, typically measured between the two **half-power (−3 dB) points**.

It is mathematically expressed as:

$$
BW = f_H - f_L
$$

where $f_H$ is the upper cutoff frequency and $f_L$ is the lower cutoff frequency. For audio amplifiers, $f_H \gg f_L$, so $BW \approx f_H$. The amplifier passes all frequencies within the bandwidth with gain close to the mid-band value and attenuates frequencies outside this range.

**[Distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for approximate relation and significance.]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

What is the **Miller effect**, and how does it affect the upper cutoff frequency of a CE amplifier?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Miller effect** (or **Miller multiplication**) refers to the apparent increase in the effective capacitance between the input and output of an inverting amplifier due to the **gain multiplication** of the feedback capacitance. The base-collector capacitance $C_{bc}$ of a BJT appears at the input as a much larger value:

$$
C_M = C_{bc}(1 + A_v)
$$

This increased effective input capacitance reduces the upper cutoff frequency, since:

$$
f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} (C_{be} + C_M)}
$$

Thus, **higher gain results in lower upper cutoff frequency**, limiting the bandwidth of high-gain CE stages.

**[Distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for implication on $f_H$.]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **single-stage RC coupled CE amplifier**. Discuss the function of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **mid-band voltage gain** and **lower cutoff frequency** of the amplifier. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Circuit Description and Operation (7 marks):**

The single-stage RC coupled CE amplifier consists of:
- **Transistor Q1 (BC547 NPN BJT)** — the active amplifying device.
- **$R_1$ and $R_2$** — voltage divider bias resistors that set the DC base bias voltage.
- **$R_E$** — emitter resistor for **DC bias stabilization** through negative feedback.
- **$C_E$** — emitter bypass capacitor that shorts $R_E$ for AC signals, preventing AC negative feedback.
- **$R_C$** — collector resistor that converts the collector current variations into output voltage variations.
- **$C_{in}$** — input coupling capacitor that blocks DC from the source and passes only AC.
- **$C_{out}$** — output coupling capacitor that blocks DC collector voltage and delivers AC to the load $R_L$.
- **$V_{CC} = +12\text{ V}$** — DC supply.
