**Operation:** The small AC input signal $V_{in}$ is applied at the base via $C_{in}$. The transistor amplifies this signal and produces an amplified, **phase-inverted (180°)** output at the collector. The DC component is blocked by $C_{out}$, and the AC output drives the load $R_L$.

The DC operating point (Q-point) is established by $R_1$, $R_2$, and $R_E$ to keep the transistor in the **active region** for all input signal swings. The AC equivalent circuit is obtained by short-circuiting $V_{CC}$ and replacing the BJT with its small-signal hybrid-π model.

**(b) Derivation of Mid-band Gain and Lower Cutoff Frequency (7 marks):**

**Mid-band Voltage Gain:**

In the mid-band region, $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$, and $C_E$ are AC short circuits. The BJT is replaced by its hybrid-π model. The small-signal transconductance is:

$$
g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T}
$$

The AC load at the collector is $R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L$. The output voltage is:

$$
V_{out} = -g_m V_{be} R_{ac}
$$

Since $V_{in} \approx V_{be}$:

$$
A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -g_m R_{ac}
$$

**Lower Cutoff Frequency:**

The lower cutoff frequency is determined by the high-pass RC networks formed by the coupling and bypass capacitors. Each capacitor contributes a pole:

$$
f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_{in}}
$$

$$
f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L) C_{out}}
$$

$$
f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_E C_E}
$$

Using the Bode approximation for non-interacting poles:

$$
f_L \approx f_{L1} + f_{L2} + f_{L3}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Draw the **frequency response curve** of a typical RC coupled CE amplifier and explain its **three regions** with relevant equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Miller effect** in detail. How does it influence the **upper cutoff frequency** and **gain-bandwidth product** of a CE amplifier? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Frequency Response Curve (7 marks):**

The frequency response curve is plotted on a **semi-log scale** with frequency (Hz) on the x-axis (logarithmic) and gain (dB) on the y-axis (linear). The curve has a **band-pass shape** with three regions:

- **Region I — Low Frequency ($f < f_L$):** Gain rolls off at **+20 dB/decade** because the reactance of coupling and bypass capacitors becomes large. Gain equation:

$$
A_v = \frac{A_{v(\text{mid})}}{\sqrt{1 + (f_L / f)^2}}
$$

- **Region II — Mid-Frequency ($f_L \le f \le f_H$):** Gain is **constant and maximum** at $A_{v(\text{mid})}$ in dB, because all capacitors are effectively short/open as required. Gain equation:

$$
A_v = A_{v(\text{mid})} = -g_m R_{ac}
$$

- **Region III — High Frequency ($f > f_H$):** Gain rolls off at **−20 dB/decade** because of internal transistor capacitances and the Miller effect. Gain equation:

$$
A_v = \frac{A_{v(\text{mid})}}{\sqrt{1 + (f / f_H)^2}}
$$

The bandwidth is $BW = f_H - f_L$.

**(b) Miller Effect (7 marks):**

The **Miller effect** describes the multiplication of the feedback capacitance $C_{bc}$ (between base and collector of a CE BJT) by a factor of $(1 + A_v)$ when viewed from the input side.

**Derivation:** In the high-frequency small-signal model, $C_{bc}$ connects the base node (voltage $V_b$) to the collector node (voltage $V_c$). The current through $C_{bc}$ is:

$$
I = j\omega C_{bc} (V_b - V_c) = j\omega C_{bc} V_b (1 + A_v)
$$

This is equivalent to a capacitance at the input node:

$$
C_M = C_{bc} (1 + A_v)
$$

**Influence on $f_H$:** The total input capacitance becomes $C_{be} + C_M$, and:

$$
f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} (C_{be} + C_{bc}(1 + A_v))}
$$

Thus, **higher voltage gain reduces the upper cutoff frequency**.

**Influence on Gain-Bandwidth Product:**

$$
\text{GBP} = A_v \cdot f_H = \frac{A_v}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{be} + 2\pi R_{eq} C_{bc} A_v} \approx \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{bc}}
$$

The GBP is approximately **constant for a given transistor**, indicating a fundamental trade-off: increasing gain decreases bandwidth and vice versa.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 180° phase inversion:** CE amplifiers invert the signal. Marks are lost if students write $A_v = +g_m R_{ac}$ instead of $A_v = -g_m R_{ac}$.
>
> 2. **Ignoring the $V_T = 26\text{ mV}$ thermal voltage:** When calculating $g_m = I_C / V_T$, students often omit the value of $V_T$ or use 25 mV. The standard KTU expectation is **26 mV at 300 K** unless otherwise specified.
>
> 3. **Confusing $f_L$ formulas:** Some students write $f_L = 1/(2\pi R C)$ for a single capacitor and forget that the **total $f_L$ is the sum of contributions** from all three capacitors (Bode approximation).
>
> 4. **Miller effect — wrong direction:** Students sometimes apply the Miller effect to $C_{be}$ instead of $C_{bc}$. Only the **feedback capacitance** ($C_{bc}$) is multiplied by $(1 + A_v)$.
>
> 5. **Units inconsistency:** Always express $C$ in **Farads** (convert $\mu$F to $10^{-6}$ F, pF to $10^{-12}$ F) before substituting into the formula.
>
> 6. **Neglecting the BJT internal capacitances in mid-band:** In the mid-band analysis, $C_{be}$ and $C_{bc}$ are **open circuits**, not short circuits. Only the **external** coupling and bypass capacitors are short circuits in mid-band.
>
> 7. **Bandwidth formula:** Writing $BW = f_H + f_L$ instead of $BW = f_H - f_L$ will fetch **zero marks** for that sub-part.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — RC Coupled CE Amplifier Frequency Response**
>
> - **RC Coupled CE Amplifier:** A multi-stage amplifier using **Resistor-Capacitor coupling** between stages. Most common coupling method for audio amplifiers.
>
> - **Three Frequency Regions:**
>   - **Low frequency** ($f < f_L$): Gain rolls off at **+20 dB/decade** due to coupling and bypass capacitors.
>   - **Mid frequency** ($f_L \le f \le f_H$): Gain is **constant and maximum** = $-g_m R_{ac}$.
>   - **High frequency** ($f > f_H$): Gain rolls off at **−20 dB/decade** due to Miller effect and transistor internal capacitances.
>
> - **Mid-band Gain:** $A_v = -g_m R_{ac}$, with $g_m = I_C / 26\text{ mV}$ at 300 K and $R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L$.
>
> - **Gain in dB:** $A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$.
>
> - **Lower Cutoff:** Sum of three RC pole contributions — input coupling, output coupling, and emitter bypass.
>
> - **Upper Cutoff:** $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} [C_{be} + C_{bc}(1 + A_v)]}$, dominated by Miller capacitance.
>
> - **Bandwidth:** $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$.
>
> - **Gain-Bandwidth Product:** Approximately **constant** for a given transistor.
>
> - **180° Phase Shift:** CE configuration inverts the input signal — a defining feature.
>
> - **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
>   - $g_m = I_C / V_T$, where $V_T = 26\text{ mV}$.
>   - $C_M = C_{bc}(1 + A_v)$.
>   - $f_L \approx \sum f_{Li}$, $f_H = 1/(2\pi R_{eq} C_{\text{in,total}})$.
>   - $BW = f_H - f_L$, $\text{GBP} = A_v \cdot BW$.
>
> - **Experimental Tip:** When plotting the frequency response in the lab, use **semi-log graph paper**, mark the **−3 dB points** clearly, and compute the bandwidth by reading $f_H - f_L$ from the curve.
>
> - **Practical Limits:** Typical audio amplifier: $f_L \approx 20\text{ Hz}$, $f_H \approx 20\text{ kHz}$, mid-band gain $\approx 40\text{ dB}$ per stage.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Low Frequency Oscillators - RC Phase Shift or Wien Bridge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL308 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUIT AND LOGIC DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Low Frequency Oscillators - RC Phase Shift or Wien Bridge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Low Frequency Oscillator?

A **low frequency oscillator (LFO)** is an electronic circuit that generates a periodic, repeating waveform (typically sinusoidal) at frequencies in the range of **1 Hz to 100 kHz**. These oscillators are widely used as laboratory signal sources, in audio applications, function generators, and instrumentation systems where high-purity sine waves at low frequencies are required.

In the context of KTU PCBRL308, this experiment focuses on **RC-based sinusoidal oscillators**, where the frequency of oscillation is determined by **resistor (R) and capacitor (C) values** rather than by inductors (which would make it an LC oscillator suitable for higher frequencies).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *An oscillator is a circuit that converts DC energy from the supply into AC energy at a specific frequency without requiring an external input signal, sustained by positive feedback satisfying the **Barkhausen Criterion**.*

## 1.2 The Two Variants Studied in This Experiment

| Oscillator Type | Amplifier Used | Phase Shift by RC Network | Frequency Range |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **RC Phase Shift Oscillator** | Inverting (CE BJT / Op-Amp) | 180° from 3 RC sections | **1 Hz – 100 kHz** |
| **Wien Bridge Oscillator** | Non-Inverting (Op-Amp) | 0° from lead-lag network | **1 Hz – 1 MHz** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Swinging Pendulum

Imagine a grandfather clock's pendulum: once you give it a small push, the escapement mechanism provides a tiny push at just the right moment during each swing. The pendulum doesn't need a continuous push — it only needs a *timed, in-phase nudge* to keep going indefinitely.

An oscillator works exactly like this:
- The **amplifier** provides the energy (the "push").
- The **RC feedback network** decides the **timing** (when and how much phase shift to give back).
- The **Barkhausen criterion** ensures the push is always in the right direction at the right time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** An oscillator is essentially a tuned amplifier with a portion of its output cleverly fed back to its input in the correct phase, so the circuit "ticks" itself forever.

## 1.4 The Barkhausen Criterion (Foundation Concept)

For sustained oscillations, two simultaneous conditions must be met:

1. **Loop Gain Magnitude Condition:** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ — the product of amplifier gain (A) and feedback factor ($\beta$) must equal exactly **unity**.
2. **Loop Phase Condition:** $\angle A\beta = 0° \text{ (or } 360°\text{)}$ — the total phase shift around the loop must be **zero (or an integer multiple of 360°)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At the moment of switch-on, the loop gain must be slightly **greater than 1** (e.g., $|A\beta| = 1.01$) so that the oscillations build up. Once the desired amplitude is reached, automatic amplitude stabilization (AGC) reduces the effective gain back to **exactly 1**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 RC Phase Shift Oscillator — Operational Logic

The RC phase shift oscillator uses an **inverting amplifier** (CE BJT configuration or inverting op-amp) which inherently provides a **180° phase shift** between input and output. To satisfy the Barkhausen phase condition, the **RC feedback network** must contribute an **additional 180°**.

This 180° is obtained by cascading **three identical RC sections**, each contributing **60°** at the desired frequency of oscillation.

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic

- **Step 1:** A common-emitter (CE) amplifier provides a 180° phase inversion. This is its natural behavior — output is anti-phase to input.
- **Step 2:** The output of the amplifier is fed back to the input via a three-stage RC ladder network. Each stage is a high-pass filter (series C, shunt R).
- **Step 3:** At the **frequency of oscillation**, each RC section introduces a 60° phase lag, totaling 180°.
- **Step 4:** Total loop phase = 180° (amplifier) + 180° (RC network) = 360° ≡ 0° → Barkhausen satisfied.
- **Step 5:** For the magnitude condition, the **minimum closed-loop gain** of the CE stage must be **29** to compensate for the attenuation ($\vert\beta\vert = 1/29$) of the RC ladder.

## 2.2 Wien Bridge Oscillator — Operational Logic

The Wien bridge oscillator uses a **non-inverting amplifier** (op-amp) which provides **0° phase shift** by default. The frequency-selective feedback network must therefore contribute **0° (or 360°)** at the desired frequency.

The Wien network is a **lead-lag circuit** composed of:
- **Series arm:** $R$ in series with $C$ → behaves as a lead network at high frequencies.
- **Shunt arm:** $R$ in parallel with $C$ → behaves as a lag network at high frequencies.

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic

- **Step 1:** A non-inverting op-amp (with gain $A = 1 + R_f/R_i$) provides 0° phase shift.
- **Step 2:** The Wien network is a frequency-selective voltage divider. The series arm ($Z_s$) and shunt arm ($Z_p$) form an AC bridge.
- **Step 3:** At the **resonant frequency** $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$, the reactance of C equals R, and the bridge is **balanced**.
- **Step 4:** The feedback fraction is $\beta = 1/3$ at this frequency, requiring a non-inverting gain of **exactly 3** to satisfy $\vert A\beta\vert = 1$.
- **Step 5:** Amplitude stabilization is achieved using **non-linear elements** (back-to-back Zener diodes, thermistor, or JFET) in the feedback path.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | RC Phase Shift Oscillator | Wien Bridge Oscillator |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Frequency of Oscillation** | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ |
| 2 | **Angular Frequency** | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}}$ | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ |
| 3 | **Feedback Factor** $\beta$ | $\dfrac{1}{29}$ | $\dfrac{1}{3}$ |
| 4 | **Minimum Loop Gain** $\vert A\beta\vert = 1$ | $\vert A_v\vert = 29$ | $\vert A_v\vert = 3$ |
| 5 | **Phase Shift by RC Network** | $180°$ (3 × 60°) | $0°$ |
| 6 | **Phase Shift by Amplifier** | $180°$ (Inverting CE) | $0°$ (Non-Inverting) |
| 7 | **Design Constraint** | $R_C \geq 29 R_E$ for BJT CE | $R_f = 2 R_i$ for op-amp |
| 8 | **Stability of Frequency** | Moderate (depends on 6 RC values) | High (depends on only 2 RC values) |
| 9 | **Typical Frequency Range** | **1 Hz – 100 kHz** | **1 Hz – 1 MHz** |
| 10 | **Output Waveform Purity** | Moderate (harmonic distortion $\approx 1\%$) | High (with amplitude control) |

> [!NOTE]
> The values **29** and **3** are the *minimum* required gains. In practice, gain is set slightly higher (e.g., 30 for RC phase shift, 3.2 for Wien bridge) to ensure oscillations build up reliably from noise. Amplitude control circuitry (thermistor / diodes / JFET) then reduces the effective gain to exactly the required value.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **RC Phase Shift:** Used in simple, low-cost sinusoidal signal generators, audio tone generators, and educational lab kits. Popular for demonstrating oscillator theory.
- **Wien Bridge:** The **industry standard** for high-purity sine wave generation. Used in commercial function/signal generators (e.g., HP/Agilent 33120A), audio test equipment, and instrumentation because of its excellent frequency stability and low distortion.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Frequency of RC Phase Shift Oscillator

### Step 1 — Define the Feedback Network

Consider a single RC section (high-pass type) where $R$ is the series resistor and $C$ is the shunt capacitor. The transfer function of a single section is:

$$
\frac{V_{o1}}{V_{in}} = \frac{R}{R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}} = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}
$$

**Logic:** This is a high-pass filter — it passes high frequencies and attenuates low frequencies, with a phase shift that varies from $+90°$ (at low $\omega$) to $0°$ (at high $\omega$).

### Step 2 — Cascade Three Identical Sections (but not directly!)

In the **RC phase shift oscillator**, the three sections are **NOT simply cascaded** because the loading of one section affects the next. The standard analysis assumes the next stage (the amplifier input) draws negligible current (high input impedance), so the sections can be analyzed as **non-loading cascaded**. We can also use the **mesh/nodal analysis** of the entire ladder.

The complete feedback network consists of three sections: $R_1 C_1$, $R_2 C_2$, $R_3 C_3$ — but for the standard derivation, $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C_3 = C$.

### Step 3 — Use Standard Nodal Analysis

Let the output of the amplifier be $V_o$, and the input to the amplifier be $V_i$ (which is also the output of the RC ladder). Apply KCL at each node of the ladder. After considerable algebraic manipulation, the feedback factor is:

$$
\beta(j\omega) = \frac{1}{\left(1 - 5\omega^2 R^2 C^2\right) + j\left(6\omega RC - \omega^3 R^3 C^3\right)}
$$

**Logic:** This is the standard form obtained by setting up three nodal equations and solving for the ratio $V_i/V_o$.

### Step 4 — Apply the Phase Condition (Barkhausen)

For oscillation, $\beta$ must be **real and negative** (so the amplifier's $180°$ inversion makes the total $360°$). This requires the **imaginary part of the denominator to be zero**:

$$
6\omega RC - \omega^3 R^3 C^3 = 0
$$

Factor out $\omega RC$:

$$
\omega RC \left(6 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2\right) = 0
$$

Since $\omega RC \neq 0$ (we want oscillation, not DC), we have:

$$
6 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 0
$$

$$
\omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 6
$$

$$
\omega RC = \sqrt{6}
$$

### Step 5 — Solve for Frequency

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}}
$$

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}
$$

### Step 6 — Apply the Magnitude Condition

Substitute $\omega_0 RC = \sqrt{6}$ into the real part of the denominator:

$$
\text{Denominator (real part)} = 1 - 5\omega_0^2 R^2 C^2 = 1 - 5(6) = 1 - 30 = -29
$$

Therefore:

$$
\beta = \frac{1}{-29} = -\frac{1}{29}
$$

The negative sign confirms $180°$ phase shift. For $\vert A\beta\vert = 1$:

$$
\vert A_v \vert = \frac{1}{\vert\beta\vert} = 29
$$

**Minimum required gain = 29** (BJT CE amplifier must have $R_C \geq 29 R_E$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Frequency of Wien Bridge Oscillator

### Step 1 — Define the Wien Network Impedances

The Wien network consists of:
- **Series arm impedance:** $Z_s = R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C} = \dfrac{1 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C}$
- **Parallel arm impedance:** $Z_p = R \parallel \dfrac{1}{j\omega C} = \dfrac{R \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}} = \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$

### Step 2 — Form the Voltage Divider (Feedback Factor)

The Wien network acts as a voltage divider between $Z_s$ and $Z_p$. The feedback factor is:

$$
\beta = \frac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}
$$

Substitute:

$$
\beta = \frac{\dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}{\dfrac{1 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C} + \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}
$$

### Step 3 — Simplify the Denominator

Multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega C (1 + j\omega RC)$:

$$
\beta = \frac{j\omega RC \cdot R}{(1 + j\omega RC)^2 + j\omega RC}
$$

### Step 4 — Expand the Denominator

$$
(1 + j\omega RC)^2 = 1 + 2j\omega RC + (j\omega RC)^2 = 1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 + 2j\omega RC
$$

So the denominator becomes:

$$
(1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 + 2j\omega RC) + j\omega RC = (1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2) + 3j\omega RC
$$

Therefore:

$$
\beta = \frac{j\omega RC}{(1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2) + 3j\omega RC}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Phase Condition (Barkhausen)

For a non-inverting amplifier, $\beta$ must be **real and positive** (so the loop phase = 0°). This requires the **imaginary part of $\beta$ to be zero**. Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate:

$$
\beta = \frac{j\omega RC \cdot \left[(1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2) - 3j\omega RC\right]}{(1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2)^2 + 9\omega^2 R^2 C^2}
$$

$$
\beta = \frac{j\omega RC (1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2) + 3\omega^2 R^2 C^2}{(1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2)^2 + 9\omega^2 R^2 C^2}
$$

The **imaginary part** is $\omega RC(1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2)$. Setting this to zero:

$$
\omega RC(1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2) = 0
$$

Since $\omega RC \neq 0$:

$$
1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 0
$$

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC}
$$

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}
$$

### Step 6 — Apply the Magnitude Condition

At $\omega_0 RC = 1$, the denominator becomes:

$$
(1 - 1)^2 + 9(1)^2 = 0 + 9 = 9
$$

The numerator becomes:

$$
3\omega_0^2 R^2 C^2 = 3(1)^2 = 3
$$

So:

$$
\beta = \frac{3}{9} = \frac{1}{3}
$$

For $\vert A\beta\vert = 1$:

$$
\vert A_v \vert = 3
$$

Since the non-inverting op-amp gain is $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_i}$:

$$
1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i} = 3 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R_f = 2 R_i
$$

**This is the design condition for a Wien bridge oscillator using an op-amp.**

---

## 3.3 Design Calculation Worked Example (KTU Typical Exam)

> **Problem:** Design a Wien bridge oscillator using an op-amp to generate a **1 kHz** sine wave. Choose $C = 10 \text{ nF}$ and find the required $R$ and feedback resistor values.

**Solution:**

Given: $f_0 = 1000 \text{ Hz}$, $C = 10 \text{ nF} = 10 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F}$.

From the formula:

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}
$$

Solve for $R$:

$$
R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_0 C}
$$

$$
R = \frac{1}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 1000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
R = \frac{1}{6.2832 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 15.92 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

Choose the nearest standard value: $R = 15 \text{ k}\Omega$ (or 16 kΩ).

For the non-inverting amplifier: $R_f = 2 R_i$.

Choose $R_i = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, then $R_f = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$.

> For **practical stability**, choose $R_f = 20 \text{ k}\Omega + 5 \text{ k}\Omega$ (trimmer potentiometer) so that gain can be set slightly above 3 to start oscillations, with amplitude control elements (Zener diodes / thermistor) providing automatic gain regulation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RC Phase Shift Oscillator — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC[VCC Supply] --> BIAS[Bias Network]
    VEE[VEE Supply] --> BIAS
    BIAS --> AMP1[CE Amplifier BJT]
    IN1[AC Input] --> AMP1
    AMP1 --> OUT1[Sine Output Vout]
    AMP1 --> RC1[RC Section 1 - 60 Deg]
    RC1 --> RC2[RC Section 2 - 60 Deg]
    RC2 --> RC3[RC Section 3 - 60 Deg]
    RC3 --> FB[Feedback to Input]
    FB --> IN1
    AMP1 -.->|Gain must be 29| GCTRL[Gain Control RC greater than 29 RE]
```

**Description of Flow:** The CE amplifier provides 180° phase shift and the cascaded three RC sections collectively introduce 180° more, completing the Barkhausen loop. The total loop gain equals 1 only when the amplifier's gain is at least 29.

## 4.2 Wien Bridge Oscillator — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VCC2[DC Supply VCC] --> OP1[Op-Amp 741]
    VEE2[DC Supply VEE] --> OP1
    OP1 --> OUT2[Sine Output Vout]
    OUT2 --> ZS[Series Arm Zs = R + 1 over j omega C]
    ZS --> ZP[Shunt Arm Zp = R parallel 1 over j omega C]
    ZP --> GND1[Ground]
    ZP --> FB2[Feedback Node Vfb]
    FB2 --> RIN[Resistor Ri to Inverting Input]
    OP1 -.->|Non-inverting input| NINV[Plus Terminal]
    NINV --> FB2
    RF[Feedback Resistor Rf] --> OP1
    OP1 --> RF
    RF -.->|Gain equals 3| AMPCTL[Amplitude Control Zener or Thermistor]
    AMPCTL --> RIN
```

**Description of Flow:** The op-amp provides 0° phase shift. The Wien network (Zs in series with Zp) feeds back a fraction of the output. At the resonant frequency, the bridge balances and exactly 1/3 of the output is fed back, requiring a non-inverting gain of 3.

## 4.3 Frequency-Selective Network Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RC_PSO[RC PHASE SHIFT NETWORK]
        A1[Vin] --> A2[R1]
        A2 --> A3[Node X]
        A3 --> A4[C1 to Ground]
        A3 --> A5[R2]
        A5 --> A6[Node Y]
        A6 --> A7[C2 to Ground]
        A6 --> A8[R3]
        A8 --> A9[Node Z]
        A9 --> A10[C3 to Ground]
        A9 --> A11[Vout to Amplifier Input]
    end
    subgraph WBO[WIEN BRIDGE NETWORK]
        B1[Vin from Amplifier Output] --> B2[R Series]
        B2 --> B3[Node M]
        B3 --> B4[C Series]
        B4 --> B5[Node N]
        B5 --> B6[Ground]
        B5 --> B7[R Parallel]
        B7 --> B8[Node N]
        B8 --> B9[C Parallel]
        B9 --> B10[Ground]
        B5 --> B11[Vout to Amplifier Non-inverting Input]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above diagrams are **functional flow representations**. In the actual lab experiment, you will draw the **circuit schematic** by hand in your record, including component pin numbers (e.g., IC 741 pin 2, pin 3, pin 6), supply decoupling capacitors ($0.1 \mu F$ across V+ and V-), and amplitude stabilization circuitry.

## 4.4 Typical Lab Wiring Overview

| Component | RC Phase Shift | Wien Bridge |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Active Device | BJT (BC107/2N2222) or Op-Amp | Op-Amp IC 741 |
| Frequency-Determining Components | 3 × R, 3 × C | 2 × R, 2 × C |
| Feedback Path | RC ladder to base / +input | Wien network to +input |
| Amplitude Stabilization | Not always included | Zener / thermistor / JFET |
| Output Observation | CRO (CH1: input, CH2: output) | CRO (CH1: input, CH2: output) |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the Barkhausen criterion for sustained oscillations. Mention the two conditions that must be satisfied.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Barkhausen criterion specifies two conditions for sustained oscillations in a feedback amplifier:

1. **Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain $\vert A\beta\vert$ must equal **unity** (1). This ensures that the signal circulates around the loop without growing or decaying.

2. **Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the closed loop must be **0° (or an integer multiple of 360°)**. This ensures that the feedback signal is in phase with the original input, reinforcing it.

For initial build-up, $\vert A\beta\vert$ must be slightly greater than 1; for steady-state, it must equal exactly 1.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is a Wien bridge oscillator preferred over an RC phase shift oscillator for generating low-frequency sinusoidal signals? Mention any two reasons.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Wien bridge oscillator is preferred over the RC phase shift oscillator for the following reasons:

1. **Frequency Stability:** The Wien bridge uses only **two frequency-determining components** (R and C), making it less sensitive to component variations. The RC phase shift oscillator depends on **six components** (3R and 3C), where any small drift causes significant frequency changes.

2. **Pure Sine Wave Output:** The Wien bridge provides a much **purer sinusoidal output** with very low harmonic distortion (less than 0.1%) because of the use of amplitude stabilization circuits (thermistor/Zener diodes). The RC phase shift oscillator has higher distortion (around 1–2%).

3. **Simpler Gain Requirement:** The Wien bridge requires a non-inverting gain of only **3**, which is easy to design with a single op-amp, whereas the RC phase shift oscillator needs a gain of **29** with proper biasing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — RC Phase Shift Oscillator

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an RC phase shift oscillator using a BJT. Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation and the condition for sustained oscillations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design an RC phase shift oscillator using a BJT to generate a sine wave of frequency **2 kHz**. Assume all capacitors are **10 nF** and find the values of all three resistors. Also determine the required value of the emitter resistor for sustained oscillations. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Working, Derivation & Condition (7 Marks):**

The RC phase shift oscillator consists of a common-emitter (CE) amplifier with a three-stage RC feedback network connected between the collector (output) and the base (input). The CE amplifier provides 180° phase shift. Each RC section is a high-pass filter contributing a phase shift that varies from 0° to 90°. At the frequency of oscillation, each section contributes 60°, totaling 180° from the RC network. Hence the total loop phase shift becomes 180° + 180° = 360° = 0°, satisfying the Barkhausen phase condition.

The transfer function of the feedback network is:

$$
\beta(j\omega) = \frac{1}{(1 - 5\omega^2 R^2 C^2) + j(6\omega RC - \omega^3 R^3 C^3)}
$$

Setting the imaginary part to zero:

$$
6\omega RC - \omega^3 R^3 C^3 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}}
$$

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}
$$

At this frequency, the real part of the denominator becomes $1 - 5(6) = -29$, so $\beta = -1/29$. For the Barkhausen magnitude condition $\vert A\beta\vert = 1$, we require $\vert A_v\vert \geq 29$.

**Part (b) — Design Calculation (7 Marks):**

Given: $f_0 = 2000 \text{ Hz}$, $C = 10 \text{ nF} = 10^{-8} \text{ F}$.

From the frequency formula:

$$
R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_0 C \sqrt{6}}
$$

$$
R = \frac{1}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 2000 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.449}
$$

$$
R = \frac{1}{3.079 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3.25 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

So all three resistors $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 \approx 3.3 \text{ k}\Omega$ (standard value).

**For the emitter resistor:**

The CE amplifier must have a gain of at least 29. For a CE amplifier with unbypassed emitter resistor:

$$
A_v = -\frac{R_C}{R_E} \quad (\text{magnitude}) = 29
$$

Choosing $R_C = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$:

$$
R_E = \frac{R_C}{29} = \frac{10000}{29} \approx 345 \text{ }\Omega
$$

Use standard value $R_E = 330 \text{ }\Omega$ or $350 \text{ }\Omega$.

For proper biasing, the base bias resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ (the DC bias divider, NOT to be confused with the RC network resistors) form a voltage divider such that $V_{BE} \approx 0.7 \text{ V}$ and $V_E \approx 2$–$3 \text{ V}$. Choose $R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_1 = 40 \text{ k}\Omega$ for $V_{CC} = 12 \text{ V}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Wien Bridge Oscillator

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a Wien bridge oscillator using an op-amp. Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation and the minimum gain required for sustained oscillations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a Wien bridge oscillator using op-amp IC 741 to generate a sine wave of frequency **5 kHz**. Choose $C = 1 \text{ nF}$ and determine R. Also calculate the values of the feedback resistors for sustained oscillations and explain any one method of amplitude stabilization. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Working, Derivation & Gain (7 Marks):**

The Wien bridge oscillator uses a non-inverting op-amp configuration with a Wien network in the positive feedback path. The Wien network is a frequency-selective lead-lag circuit formed by a series RC combination ($Z_s = R + 1/j\omega C$) and a parallel RC combination ($Z_p = R \parallel 1/j\omega C$). The op-amp provides 0° phase shift, and at the resonant frequency, the Wien network also provides 0° shift, satisfying the phase condition.

The feedback factor is:

$$
\beta = \frac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p} = \frac{j\omega RC}{(1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2) + 3j\omega RC}
$$

Setting the imaginary part to zero (for real, positive $\beta$):

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}
$$

At this frequency, $\beta = 1/3$. For the magnitude condition $\vert A\beta\vert = 1$:

$$
A_v = 3
$$

**Part (b) — Design Calculation (7 Marks):**

Given: $f_0 = 5000 \text{ Hz}$, $C = 1 \text{ nF} = 10^{-9} \text{ F}$.

$$
R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_0 C} = \frac{1}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 5000 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 31.83 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

Use standard value: $R = 33 \text{ k}\Omega$ (a small trimmer of 30 kΩ in series with 5 kΩ pot is used for fine frequency adjustment).

For non-inverting gain of 3:

$$
A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i} = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_f = 2 R_i
$$

Choose $R_i = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, then $R_f = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**Amplitude Stabilization Method (any one):**

**Method 1 — Back-to-Back Zener Diodes:** Connect two Zener diodes (e.g., 5.1 V each) in anti-parallel across $R_f$. When output amplitude tries to exceed the Zener voltage, the diodes conduct, effectively reducing $R_f$ and hence the gain. This brings the loop gain back to exactly 1, stabilizing the amplitude.

**Method 2 — Thermistor (NTC) in Place of $R_E$:** Use a negative temperature coefficient thermistor as the lower feedback resistor. As amplitude increases, the thermistor heats up, its resistance decreases, gain drops, and amplitude is limited.

**Method 3 — JFET as Voltage-Controlled Resistor:** Use a JFET in the feedback path with its gate controlled by the output (rectified and filtered). Increased output amplitude reduces JFET's $R_{DS}$, lowering gain.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Wrong frequency formula:** Writing $f = 1/(2\pi RC)$ for the **RC Phase Shift Oscillator** (it should be $1/(2\pi RC\sqrt{6})$). Examiners specifically test whether you know the difference between the two oscillators.
>
> 2. **Wrong gain value:** Writing minimum gain as 3 for RC phase shift, or 29 for Wien bridge. Memorize the pair: **RC Phase Shift = 29**, **Wien Bridge = 3**.
>
> 3. **Missing phase analysis:** Students often write only the magnitude condition and forget to state the phase condition. Both are required for **full marks** in Part B.
>
> 4. **Component labeling confusion:** In the RC phase shift, $R_1, R_2, R_3$ of the feedback ladder are **different** from the bias resistors $R_1, R_2$ of the BJT base divider. Always label clearly.
>
> 5. **No amplitude stabilization discussed in Wien Bridge:** For sustained stable oscillations, you MUST mention the amplitude control mechanism (Zener, thermistor, or JFET). Failure to do so costs 2–3 marks.
>
> 6. **Not sketching the circuit diagram:** Always draw a **neat, labeled circuit diagram** with all components marked, including the supply rails and the load. A question without a diagram loses at least 1–2 marks in the KTU valuation scheme.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Barkhausen Criterion:** Two conditions — (1) $\vert A\beta\vert = 1$ and (2) total loop phase shift = 0° or 360°.
- **RC Phase Shift Oscillator:**
    - Uses **inverting amplifier** (CE BJT) → 180° phase shift.
    - Uses **3 RC sections** in feedback path → another 180° (60° + 60° + 60°).
    - **Frequency formula:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$.
    - **Minimum gain:** $\vert A_v\vert = 29$.
    - **Design rule:** $R_C \geq 29 R_E$ for the BJT CE stage.
- **Wien Bridge Oscillator:**
    - Uses **non-inverting op-amp** → 0° phase shift.
    - Uses **Wien network** (series RC + parallel RC) in positive feedback → 0° at resonance.
    - **Frequency formula:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$.
    - **Minimum gain:** $A_v = 3$, i.e., $R_f = 2 R_i$.
    - **Amplitude stabilization required** (Zener / thermistor / JFET).
- **Stability Comparison:** Wien bridge is **far more stable** than RC phase shift because it has fewer frequency-determining components (2 vs 6).
- **Output Purity:** Wien bridge produces **purer sine waves** (lower THD) than RC phase shift.
- **Lab Practical Tips:**
    - Always use a **dual power supply** ($\pm 12$ V) for the op-amp.
    - Decouple the supply with **$0.1 \mu F$** capacitors near the IC.
    - Verify output on a **CRO** — measure peak-to-peak voltage and time period.
    - Calculate frequency from the CRO waveform: $f = 1/T$.
    - Compare calculated and observed frequencies; report the **percentage error**.
    - For Wien bridge, gently **adjust the $50 \text{ k}\Omega$ potentiometer** in the feedback to find the exact point of cleanest sine wave.
- **Key Numerical Values to Memorize:**
    - $\sqrt{6} \approx 2.449$
    - For RC phase shift: $f_0 \approx \dfrac{0.065}{RC}$
    - For Wien bridge: $f_0 \approx \dfrac{0.159}{RC}$
- **Common Exam Trap:** Students often confuse $\beta$ with the transistor current gain $\beta_{DC}$. In oscillator theory, $\beta$ is the **feedback factor**, NOT the transistor's $h_{FE}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedback Amplifiers (Current Series, Voltage Series) - Gain and Frequency Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL308 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUIT AND LOGIC DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Feedback Amplifiers (Current Series, Voltage Series) - Gain and Frequency Response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Feedback Amplifier

A **Feedback Amplifier** is a two-port electronic amplifier circuit in which a portion of the output signal (voltage or current) is routed back to the input through a passive network called the **feedback network (β-network)**, and this returned signal is combined with the external input source using either series mixing or shunt (parallel) mixing. The fundamental purpose of feedback is to trade a small amount of gain for vastly superior performance characteristics such as gain stability, bandwidth extension, controlled input/output impedances, and reduced distortion.

Mathematically, the **closed-loop gain (Af)** of a negative feedback amplifier is universally expressed as:

$$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

where:
- **A** = Open-loop gain of the basic amplifier (without feedback)
- **β** = Feedback ratio (gain of the feedback network)
- **(1 + Aβ)** = **Amount of feedback** or **Loop gain sensitivity factor** or **Desensitivity factor (D)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** The term "Negative Feedback" implies that the feedback signal opposes the input signal, making the term (1 + Aβ) always positive and greater than 1. The KTU Board examiner expects the student to know that *negative feedback reduces gain but improves all other performance metrics*.

### 1.2 The Four Canonical Feedback Topologies

Feedback amplifiers are classified based on **two binary decisions**:

| Decision Axis | Options | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Sampling at Output** | Voltage Sampling (Shunt at output) | Feedback samples output **voltage** |
| **Sampling at Output** | Current Sampling (Series at output) | Feedback samples output **current** |
| **Mixing at Input** | Series Mixing | Feedback signal appears in **series** with input source |
| **Mixing at Input** | Shunt Mixing | Feedback signal appears in **shunt** (parallel) with input source |

The four resulting topologies are:
1. **Voltage-Series** (Series-Shunt) — Ideal Voltage Amplifier
2. **Current-Series** (Series-Series) — Ideal Transconductance Amplifier
3. **Voltage-Shunt** (Shunt-Shunt) — Ideal Transresistance Amplifier
4. **Current-Shunt** (Shunt-Series) — Ideal Current Amplifier

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Cruise Control System

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Cruise Control in a Car:**
> 
> Imagine driving a car without cruise control. You press the accelerator, and small bumps on the road change your speed. Now, you set cruise control to **100 km/h**. A sensor constantly measures your actual speed and feeds it back to a computer. If the car slows down on a hill, the computer pushes the accelerator harder; if it speeds up downhill, the computer reduces the accelerator. 
> 
> Here:
> - **Input** = Desired speed setting
> - **Output** = Actual car speed
> - **Feedback (β)** = Speed sensor
> - **Error signal** = (Desired speed − Actual speed)
> - **Loop gain (1+Aβ)** = Sensitivity of the correction
> 
> Just as cruise control **sacrifices responsiveness for stability**, negative feedback **sacrifices raw gain for predictable, stable, and distortion-free performance**.

### 1.4 Why is Feedback Necessary in Electronic Circuit Design?

| Without Feedback | With Negative Feedback |
|---|---|
| Gain depends heavily on transistor β, temperature, supply voltage | Gain depends almost entirely on passive components (resistors) |
| Narrow bandwidth | Bandwidth increases by factor (1 + Aβ) |
| High harmonic distortion | Distortion reduced by factor (1 + Aβ) |
| Uncontrolled input/output impedances | Precisely settable input/output impedances |
| Sensitive to noise and component aging | Robust, predictable, and reproducible behavior |

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Boltzmann's constant:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K (used in noise analysis of feedback stages)
- **Thermal voltage:** $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K (used in BJT feedback biasing)
- **Loop gain (1 + Aβ):** Dimensionless ratio, typically **10 to 1000** in practical designs
- **Feedback factor β:** Dimensionless, typically **0.001 to 0.5**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Block Diagram Model of a Negative Feedback Amplifier

The fundamental signal-flow structure of any feedback amplifier consists of **two blocks**:
1. The **Basic Amplifier (A-block)** — provides the open-loop gain A.
2. The **Feedback Network (β-block)** — samples a portion of the output and feeds it back.

The input to the A-block is called the **error signal (X_error)**:

$$X_{error} = X_{in} - X_{feedback} = X_{in} - \beta \cdot X_{out}$$

The output is:

$$X_{out} = A \cdot X_{error} = A \cdot (X_{in} - \beta \cdot X_{out})$$

Solving for the closed-loop gain:

$$A_f = \frac{X_{out}}{X_{in}} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

### 2.2 The Four Topologies — Detailed Operation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** The *input* mixing determines whether input impedance **increases** (series) or **decreases** (shunt). The *output* sampling determines whether output impedance **decreases** (voltage) or **increases** (current). Memorize this as: **Series-Sampling Series = Increase; Shunt-Sampling Shunt = Decrease** for corresponding terminals.

#### 2.2.1 Voltage-Series Feedback (Series-Shunt Topology)

- **Samples:** Output voltage (shunt connection at output, hence "shunt" sampling — the feedback network is in parallel with the load)
- **Mixes:** In series with the input source
- **Idealized Behavior:** Perfect **Voltage Amplifier** — V_out/V_in, with infinite input resistance and zero output resistance
- **Common Realization:** Common-Collector stage (Emitter Follower) with a voltage divider feedback; or CE stage with emitter resistor (partial voltage-series)
- **Key Effects:**
  - Input resistance **increases** by factor (1 + Aβ)
  - Output resistance **decreases** by factor (1 + Aβ)
  - Voltage gain stabilized
  - Bandwidth **increases** by factor (1 + Aβ)

#### 2.2.2 Current-Series Feedback (Series-Series Topology)

- **Samples:** Output current (series connection at output — feedback network in series with the load)
- **Mixes:** In series with the input source
- **Idealized Behavior:** Perfect **Transconductance Amplifier** — I_out/V_in, with infinite input and infinite output resistance
- **Common Realization:** Common-Emitter stage with **un-bypassed emitter resistor (RE)**. The voltage developed across RE is proportional to the output collector current, giving true current-series feedback.
- **Key Effects:**
  - Input resistance **increases** by factor (1 + Aβ)
  - Output resistance **increases** by factor (1 + Aβ)
  - Transconductance gain (Gm) stabilized
  - Bandwidth **increases** by factor (1 + Aβ)
  - This is the topology most commonly used in **Discrete BJT amplifier design** for general-purpose voltage gain stages

### 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — these formulas carry 70% of the marks in any KTU feedback amplifier problem.**

| # | Parameter | Voltage-Series Feedback | Current-Series Feedback |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Closed-Loop Gain** | $A_{vf} = \dfrac{A_v}{1 + A_v \beta_v}$ | $G_{mf} = \dfrac{G_m}{1 + G_m \beta_r}$ |
| 2 | **Input Resistance** | $R_{if} = R_i (1 + A_v \beta_v)$ | $R_{if} = R_i (1 + G_m \beta_r)$ |
| 3 | **Output Resistance** | $R_{of} = \dfrac{R_o}{1 + A_v \beta_v}$ | $R_{of} = R_o (1 + G_m \beta_r)$ |
| 4 | **Upper Cutoff Frequency** | $f_{Hf} = f_H (1 + A_v \beta_v)$ | $f_{Hf} = f_H (1 + G_m \beta_r)$ |
| 5 | **Lower Cutoff Frequency** | $f_{Lf} = \dfrac{f_L}{1 + A_v \beta_v}$ | $f_{Lf} = \dfrac{f_L}{1 + G_m \beta_r}$ |
| 6 | **Bandwidth** | $BW_f = BW (1 + A_v \beta_v)$ | $BW_f = BW (1 + G_m \beta_r)$ |
| 7 | **Gain × Bandwidth (GBP)** | $GBP_f = GBP_o \cdot A$ (constant) | $GBP_f = GBP_o \cdot A$ (constant) |
| 8 | **Desensitivity** | $\dfrac{dA_f}{A_f} = \dfrac{1}{(1+A\beta)} \cdot \dfrac{dA}{A}$ | Same form |
| 9 | **Feedback Network Loading** | $R_{11}$ in series with $R_s$; $R_{22} \parallel R_L$ | $R_{11}$ in series with $R_s$; $R_{22}$ in series with $R_L$ |
| 10 | **Sampling Quantity** | Output Voltage (shunt at output) | Output Current (series at output) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KRITICAL: NEVER use vertical bars (pipes) inside table cells** to denote magnitude, as it breaks the markdown table. KTU mark sheets do not reward broken tables — they penalize them. Always use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ in LaTeX inside the cell.

### 2.4 Why Feedback is the Heart of All Modern Analog IC Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Operational Amplifiers (741, TL081, LM358):** Use all four feedback topologies internally (e.g., the input differential pair uses current-shunt feedback; the gain stage uses current-series feedback; the output stage uses voltage-series feedback)
> - **Audio Power Amplifiers:** Use deep voltage-series feedback (30–60 dB of loop gain) to achieve < 0.01% Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)
> - **RF Low-Noise Amplifiers (LNAs):** Use specific impedance-matching feedback to achieve simultaneous noise and power matching
> - **Regulated Power Supplies (78xx series):** Use voltage-shunt feedback to hold output voltage constant against line and load variations
> - **Biomedical Instrumentation (ECG, EEG):** Use current-series feedback to detect tiny currents from high-impedance electrodes

### 2.5 Effect on Gain Sensitivity and Distortion

The **sensitivity** of closed-loop gain $A_f$ to variations in open-loop gain $A$ is:

$$\frac{dA_f}{A_f} = \frac{1}{1 + A\beta} \cdot \frac{dA}{A}$$

If A changes by 50% (say, due to temperature), $A_f$ changes only by 50%/(1+Aβ) — typically less than 1% in a well-designed amplifier.

For a sinusoidal input, any **harmonic distortion** generated by the basic amplifier is reduced at the output by the same factor (1 + Aβ):

$$D_f = \frac{D_o}{1 + A\beta}$$

where $D_o$ is the open-loop distortion and $D_f$ is the closed-loop distortion.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Universal Closed-Loop Gain Expression

**Starting Point:** Consider a basic amplifier with open-loop gain A whose input is the **error signal** (difference between applied input and the feedback signal).

**Step 1:** Define the error signal as the difference between the actual input and the feedback signal.

$$X_{error} = X_{in} - X_{fb}$$

**Step 2:** Express the feedback signal as β times the output.

$$X_{fb} = \beta \cdot X_{out}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute Step 2 into Step 1.

$$X_{error} = X_{in} - \beta \cdot X_{out}$$

**Step 4:** Write the amplifier's input-output relationship using the error signal.

$$X_{out} = A \cdot X_{error} = A \cdot (X_{in} - \beta \cdot X_{out})$$

**Step 5:** Expand and collect the $X_{out}$ terms on the left side.

$$X_{out} = A \cdot X_{in} - A\beta \cdot X_{out}$$

$$X_{out} + A\beta \cdot X_{out} = A \cdot X_{in}$$

**Step 6:** Factor out $X_{out}$ on the left.

$$X_{out} \cdot (1 + A\beta) = A \cdot X_{in}$$

**Step 7:** Solve for the closed-loop gain ratio $X_{out}/X_{in}$.

$$A_f = \frac{X_{out}}{X_{in}} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **master equation** of all negative feedback amplifier analysis. KTU examiners often ask students to re-derive this as a 5-mark question.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Input Resistance with Series-Mixing Feedback

**Starting Principle:** In series mixing, the same input current $I_{in}$ flows through both the source resistance $R_s$ and the amplifier's input resistance $R_i$. The feedback voltage $V_{fb}$ opposes $V_{in}$, reducing the net voltage across $R_i$.

**Step 1:** Write KVL around the input loop.

$$V_{in} = I_{in} \cdot R_s + V_{error}$$

**Step 2:** The error voltage is the input voltage that appears across $R_i$.

$$V_{error} = I_{in} \cdot R_i$$

**Step 3:** Express the feedback voltage in terms of the output. The feedback network samples the output and produces a voltage in series with the source.

$$V_{fb} = \beta \cdot V_{out}$$

**Step 4:** With feedback, the actual voltage appearing across the amplifier's input terminals is reduced by the feedback voltage. Therefore:

$$V_{error}^{with \, fb} = V_{in} - \beta \cdot V_{out} = \frac{V_{in}}{1 + A\beta}$$

**Step 5:** Therefore, the new input current with feedback is:

$$I_{in}^{with \, fb} = \frac{V_{error}^{with \, fb}}{R_i} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_i (1 + A\beta)}$$

**Step 6:** By definition, the input resistance with feedback is $R_{if} = V_{in} / I_{in}^{with \, fb}$.

$$R_{if} = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{in} / [R_i (1 + A\beta)]} = R_i \cdot (1 + A\beta)$$

$$\boxed{R_{if} = R_i (1 + A\beta)}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Output Resistance with Voltage-Sampling Feedback

**Starting Principle:** For voltage sampling, the feedback network senses the output voltage and the feedback signal is proportional to it. To find $R_{of}$, we set the independent source to zero, apply a test voltage $V_{test}$ at the output, and find the resulting current $I_{test}$.

**Step 1:** Set the independent input source to zero ($V_{in} = 0$). The error signal is then purely the feedback.

$$V_{error} = -\beta \cdot V_{out}$$

**Step 2:** The amplifier produces an output voltage (with internal source $V_{th}$ and resistance $R_o$).

$$V_{out} = A \cdot V_{error} - I_{test} \cdot R_o = -A\beta \cdot V_{out} - I_{test} \cdot R_o$$

**Step 3:** Bring the $V_{out}$ terms together.

$$V_{out} (1 + A\beta) = -I_{test} \cdot R_o$$

**Step 4:** Since $V_{test} = V_{out}$ in this test, the output resistance is:

$$R_{of} = \frac{V_{test}}{I_{test}} = \frac{R_o}{1 + A\beta}$$

$$\boxed{R_{of} = \frac{R_o}{1 + A\beta}}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Output Resistance with Current-Sampling Feedback

**Starting Principle:** For current sampling, the feedback network senses the **load current**, and the feedback signal is proportional to this current. The analysis differs because the feedback network appears in series with the load.

**Step 1:** Set input source to zero. Apply test current $I_{test}$ at the output. The amplifier's dependent source contributes $-A \cdot V_{error} = -A \cdot (-\beta \cdot I_{out}) = A\beta \cdot I_{out}$.

**Step 2:** Apply KVL at the output node.

$$V_{test} = I_{test} \cdot R_o + A\beta \cdot I_{out}$$

**Step 3:** For current sampling, $I_{out} = I_{test}$ (the current entering the feedback network equals the current entering the load).

$$V_{test} = I_{test} \cdot R_o + A\beta \cdot I_{test} = I_{test} \cdot (R_o + A\beta \cdot R_o)$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the output resistance.

$$R_{of} = \frac{V_{test}}{I_{test}} = R_o (1 + A\beta)$$

$$\boxed{R_{of} = R_o (1 + A\beta)}$$

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Bandwidth Extension due to Negative Feedback

**Starting Principle:** A single-pole amplifier has a frequency-dependent open-loop gain given by the standard low-pass form. The dominant pole causes the gain to roll off at -20 dB/decade after $f_H$.

**Step 1:** The open-loop gain as a function of frequency for a single-pole amplifier.

$$A(f) = \frac{A_{mid}}{1 + j(f/f_H)}$$

**Step 2:** The closed-loop gain is the open-loop gain divided by (1 + Aβ). At midband, A is real and large, so 1 + Aβ is essentially real. The pole of the closed-loop transfer function occurs at the frequency where the magnitude of the open-loop gain drops such that $|A(f)| \cdot \beta = 1$.

**Step 3:** Set $|A(f_{Hf})| \cdot \beta = 1$.

$$\frac{A_{mid} \cdot \beta}{\sqrt{1 + (f_{Hf}/f_H)^2}} = 1$$

**Step 4:** Using $A_{mid} \beta \gg 1$, approximate the denominator.

$$\sqrt{1 + (f_{Hf}/f_H)^2} \approx A_{mid} \beta$$

**Step 5:** Square both sides and solve for $f_{Hf}$.

$$1 + (f_{Hf}/f_H)^2 = (A_{mid} \beta)^2$$

$$(f_{Hf}/f_H)^2 = (A_{mid} \beta)^2 - 1 \approx (A_{mid} \beta)^2$$

$$f_{Hf} \approx f_H \cdot A_{mid} \beta = f_H \cdot (1 + A_{mid}\beta)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For deep feedback where $A_{mid}\beta \gg 1$, we have $1 + A_{mid}\beta \approx A_{mid}\beta$, giving the clean result $f_{Hf} \approx f_H \cdot A_{mid}\beta$. The bandwidth is extended by the loop gain.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Worked Example

**Problem:** A common-emitter amplifier has $A_v = 200$, $R_i = 1$ kΩ, $R_o = 10$ kΩ, $f_L = 100$ Hz, $f_H = 20$ kHz, and $\beta = 0.05$. Calculate all parameters with and without feedback assuming current-series feedback.

**Step 1:** Compute the amount of feedback.

$$1 + A_v \beta = 1 + (200)(0.05) = 1 + 10 = 11$$

**Step 2:** Compute the closed-loop voltage gain (treating it as a transconductance stage, but reporting the dimensionless voltage gain).

$$A_{vf} = \frac{200}{11} \approx 18.18$$

**Step 3:** Compute the input resistance with feedback (increases for series mixing).

$$R_{if} = 1 \text{ k}\Omega \times 11 = 11 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4:** Compute the output resistance with feedback (increases for current sampling).

$$R_{of} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega \times 11 = 110 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5:** Compute the new upper cutoff frequency.

$$f_{Hf} = 20 \text{ kHz} \times 11 = 220 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 6:** Compute the new lower cutoff frequency.

$$f_{Lf} = \frac{100 \text{ Hz}}{11} \approx 9.09 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 7:** Compute the new bandwidth.

$$BW_f = f_{Hf} - f_{Lf} \approx 220 \text{ kHz} - 9.09 \text{ Hz} \approx 219.99 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 8:** Verify Gain-Bandwidth product is constant.

$$GBP_{open} = 200 \times 20 \text{ kHz} = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$$

$$GBP_{closed} = 18.18 \times 220 \text{ kHz} \approx 4 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.7 Numerical Evaluation Summary

| Parameter | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop (Current-Series) | Change Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage Gain $A_v$ | 200 | 18.18 | ÷ 11 |
| Input Resistance $R_i$ | 1 kΩ | 11 kΩ | × 11 |
| Output Resistance $R_o$ | 10 kΩ | 110 kΩ | × 11 |
| Lower Cutoff $f_L$ | 100 Hz | 9.09 Hz | ÷ 11 |
| Upper Cutoff $f_H$ | 20 kHz | 220 kHz | × 11 |
| Bandwidth BW | 19.9 kHz | 219.99 kHz | × 11 |
| GBP | 4 MHz | 4 MHz | Constant |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Universal Feedback Amplifier Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Input Source Xs] --> sumA[Subtractor]
    sumA --> ampA[Basic Amplifier A]
    ampA --> outA[Output Xout]
    outA --> fbA[Feedback Network Beta]
    fbA --> sumA
    fbA -.-> fbSample[Sampled Signal]
    ampA -.-> errSig[Error Signal Xerror]
```

**Interpretation of the Flow:**
- The input source $X_s$ enters the subtractor (summing junction).
- The subtractor computes the **error signal** $X_{error} = X_s - \beta \cdot X_{out}$.
- This error signal is amplified by the basic amplifier (gain A) to produce the output.
- A portion of the output (β fraction) is returned to the subtractor through the feedback network.
- The closed-loop gain is $A/(1 + A\beta)$.

### 4.2 Topology Comparison Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Feedback Amplifier] --> decA{Output Sampling}
    decA -->|Voltage| voltA[Voltage-Series and Voltage-Shunt]
    decA -->|Current| currA[Current-Series and Current-Shunt]
    voltA --> decB{Input Mixing}
    decB -->|Series| vsA[Voltage-Series - Voltage Amp]
    decB -->|Shunt| vshA[Voltage-Shunt - Transresistance Amp]
    currA --> decC{Input Mixing}
    decC -->|Series| csA[Current-Series - Transconductance Amp]
    decC -->|Shunt| cshA[Current-Shunt - Current Amp]
```

### 4.3 Voltage-Series Feedback — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in1[Vs] --> rs1[Rs]
    rs1 --> node1[Input Node]
    node1 --> series1[Series Mixing Junction]
    series1 --> amp1[Basic Amplifier A]
    amp1 --> node2[Output Node]
    node2 --> load1[RL]
    node2 --> fb1[Voltage Feedback Network Beta]
    fb1 --> series1
    node2 -.-> vfb[Feedback Voltage]
    series1 -.-> verr[Error Voltage Verror]
```

**Architecture Mapping:**
- The "Series Mixing Junction" corresponds to a **KVL loop** where $V_s$, the drop across $R_s$, the drop across $R_i$, and the feedback voltage $V_{fb}$ all add up.
- The "Voltage Feedback Network" is connected in **parallel with the load** (shunt at output), so it senses $V_{out}$.
- The closed-loop gain is the voltage gain $A_{vf}$.

### 4.4 Current-Series Feedback — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in2[Vs] --> rs2[Rs]
    rs2 --> node3[Input Node]
    node3 --> series2[Series Mixing Junction]
    series2 --> amp2[Basic Amplifier Gm]
    amp2 --> node4[Output Node]
    node4 --> load2[RL]
    node4 --> fb2[Current Feedback Network Beta]
    fb2 --> series2
    node4 -.-> ifb[Feedback Current]
    load2 -.-> iload[Load Current Iload]
    fb2 -.-> isense[Sensed Current]
```

**Architecture Mapping:**
- The "Current Feedback Network" is connected in **series with the load** (series at output), so the current through the feedback network equals the load current $I_{load}$.
- The feedback network produces a **voltage** proportional to $I_{load}$, which is fed back in **series** with the input source.
- The closed-loop gain is the transconductance $G_{mf} = I_{out} / V_{in}$.

### 4.5 BJT Realization: Common-Emitter with Unbypassed Emitter Resistor (Current-Series)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    baseA[Base] --> qA[Q NPN BJT]
    collA[Collector] --> rLA[RL to VCC]
    emitA[Emitter] --> rEA[RE to Ground]
    baseA --> rBA[RB1]
    rBA --> vccA[VCC]
    baseA --> rB2A[RB2]
    rB2A --> gndA[Ground]
    qA --> sigA[Input vs via Cin]
```

**Physical Mapping (Current-Series Realization):**
- The NPN transistor Q forms the basic amplifier.
- $R_L$ is the collector load.
- $R_E$ is the **un-bypassed emitter resistor** — this is the heart of the current-series feedback.
- $R_{B1}$ and $R_{B2}$ form the bias divider.
- $C_{in}$ couples the input signal to the base.
- The voltage across $R_E$ is $V_{RE} = I_E \cdot R_E \approx I_C \cdot R_E$, which is proportional to the output current — confirming **current sampling** with **series mixing**.

### 4.6 BJT Realization: Voltage-Series Feedback (Common-Collector with Voltage Divider)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    baseB[Base] --> qB[Q NPN BJT]
    collB[Collector] --> vccB[VCC direct]
    emitB[Emitter] --> rE1B[R1]
    rE1B --> gndB[Ground]
    emitB --> rE2B[R2 to Ground]
    qB --> sigB[Input vs via Cin]
    outB[Output from Emitter]
```

**Physical Mapping (Voltage-Series Realization):**
- The transistor is in **common-collector** (emitter follower) configuration.
- $R_1$ and $R_2$ form a **voltage divider** at the output, sampling the output voltage.
- The middle node of the divider returns a fraction $\beta = R_2/(R_1+R_2)$ of the output voltage back to the base.
- This is **voltage sampling** (shunt at output) with **series mixing** at the base.
- The closed-loop voltage gain approaches unity: $A_{vf} \approx 1/\beta$.

### 4.7 Frequency Response — Bode Plot Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1[Low Frequency Region] --> p2[Midband Region]
    p2 --> p3[High Frequency Region]
    p2 -.-> p4[Open Loop Gain 200]
    p2 -.-> p5[Closed Loop Gain 18.18]
    p3 -.-> p6[Open Loop fH 20 kHz]
    p3 -.-> p7[Closed Loop fHf 220 kHz]
```

**Bode Plot Mapping (Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop):**
- **Open-Loop Curve:** Midband gain 200 (46 dB), with a -3 dB roll-off at 20 kHz, and -20 dB/decade slope above.
- **Closed-Loop Curve:** Midband gain 18.18 (25.2 dB), with a -3 dB roll-off at 220 kHz.
- The two curves converge at the **loop-gain crossover frequency**, beyond which the closed-loop response eventually reverts to the open-loop -20 dB/decade slope.
- The **Gain-Bandwidth Product** is conserved: $GBP = 200 \times 20\text{ kHz} = 18.18 \times 220\text{ kHz} = 4 \text{ MHz}$.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define negative feedback. List any two advantages of a negative feedback amplifier.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
> Negative feedback is the process of feeding back a portion of the output signal to the input in such a way that the feedback signal **opposes** the applied input signal, reducing the net error input to the basic amplifier. The closed-loop gain is given by $A_f = A/(1 + A\beta)$.

**Advantages (1.5 Marks — any two):**
1. **Gain Stability:** The gain becomes nearly independent of transistor parameters, temperature, and supply voltage variations. The sensitivity is reduced by the factor (1 + Aβ).
2. **Bandwidth Extension:** Both lower and upper cutoff frequencies shift in favorable directions — $f_{Lf}$ decreases and $f_{Hf}$ increases — so the overall bandwidth increases by the factor (1 + Aβ). The gain-bandwidth product remains constant.
3. **Reduced Distortion:** Harmonic distortion and noise introduced by the basic amplifier are reduced by the factor (1 + Aβ).
4. **Controlled Impedances:** Input and output impedances can be either increased or decreased by the factor (1 + Aβ), depending on the topology (series/shunt mixing and sampling).

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between voltage-series and current-series feedback amplifiers.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | Voltage-Series Feedback | Current-Series Feedback |
|---|---|---|
| **Output Sampling** | Samples output **voltage** (feedback network in shunt with load) | Samples output **current** (feedback network in series with load) |
| **Input Mixing** | Series mixing (feedback in series with source) | Series mixing (feedback in series with source) |
| **Input Resistance $R_{if}$** | $R_i(1 + A\beta)$ — **increases** | $R_i(1 + A\beta)$ — **increases** |
| **Output Resistance $R_{of}$** | $R_o/(1 + A\beta)$ — **decreases** | $R_o(1 + A\beta)$ — **increases** |
| **Idealized Amplifier Type** | Voltage amplifier ($V_{out}/V_{in}$) | Transconductance amplifier ($I_{out}/V_{in}$) |
| **Common Realization** | Emitter follower with voltage divider | CE stage with unbypassed $R_E$ |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, derive the expression for the closed-loop gain of a negative feedback amplifier. Define the term **"amount of feedback"** and explain its significance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a voltage-series feedback amplifier, the open-loop parameters are: $A_v = 1000$, $R_i = 2$ kΩ, $R_o = 20$ kΩ, $\beta = 0.04$. Determine:
1. Amount of feedback (1 + Aβ)
2. Closed-loop voltage gain $A_{vf}$
3. Input resistance with feedback $R_{if}$
4. Output resistance with feedback $R_{of}$

If the open-loop upper cutoff frequency is $f_H = 50$ kHz, find the new upper cutoff frequency with feedback. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Draw the block diagram showing input source, subtractor, basic amplifier (gain A), output node, and feedback network (gain β) connected from output back to the subtractor.

```
    Xs ──→ (+) ──→ [A] ──→ Xout
              ↑(-)              
              │                
              [β]←──────────────┘
```

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Write the error signal equation.

$$X_{error} = X_s - X_{fb} = X_s - \beta \cdot X_{out}$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Write the amplifier output and solve algebraically.

$$X_{out} = A \cdot X_{error} = A(X_s - \beta \cdot X_{out})$$

$$X_{out}(1 + A\beta) = A \cdot X_s$$

$$A_f = \frac{X_{out}}{X_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Define and explain the **amount of feedback (1 + Aβ)**.

> The quantity (1 + Aβ) is called the **amount of feedback** or **loop gain sensitivity factor** or **desensitivity factor (D)**. Its significance:
> - It represents how much the gain is reduced from A to $A/(1+A\beta)$.
> - It quantifies the factor by which input/output impedances and bandwidth are modified.
> - It determines the factor by which distortion and noise are suppressed.
> - **Larger (1+Aβ) → better performance, but lower gain.**

---

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Compute the amount of feedback.

$$1 + A_v \beta = 1 + (1000)(0.04) = 1 + 40 = 41$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Compute the closed-loop voltage gain.

$$A_{vf} = \frac{A_v}{1 + A_v \beta} = \frac{1000}{41} \approx 24.39$$

**Step 3 (1.5 Marks):** Compute the input resistance with feedback. For **voltage-series**, $R_{if}$ **increases** by the factor (1 + Aβ).

$$R_{if} = R_i (1 + A_v \beta) = 2 \text{ k}\Omega \times 41 = 82 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 (1.5 Marks):** Compute the output resistance with feedback. For **voltage-series**, $R_{of}$ **decreases** by the factor (1 + Aβ).

$$R_{of} = \frac{R_o}{1 + A_v \beta} = \frac{20 \text{ k}\Omega}{41} \approx 487.8 \, \Omega$$

**Step 5 (2 Marks):** Compute the new upper cutoff frequency.

$$f_{Hf} = f_H (1 + A_v \beta) = 50 \text{ kHz} \times 41 = 2050 \text{ kHz} = 2.05 \text{ MHz}$$

**Final Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Amount of feedback | **41** |
| $A_{vf}$ | **24.39** |
| $R_{if}$ | **82 kΩ** |
| $R_{of}$ | **487.8 Ω** |
| $f_{Hf}$ | **2.05 MHz** |

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat circuit diagram the operation of a **Common-Emitter (CE) amplifier with unbypassed emitter resistor** as a current-series feedback amplifier. Derive the expressions for input resistance, output resistance, and closed-loop gain. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A current-series feedback amplifier has the following open-loop parameters: $A_v = 500$, $R_i = 1.5$ kΩ, $R_o = 5$ kΩ, $\beta = 0.02$, $f_L = 50$ Hz, $f_H = 25$ kHz. Calculate:
1. The closed-loop gain $A_{vf}$
2. The input and output resistances with feedback
3. The new lower and upper cutoff frequencies
4. Comment on the gain-bandwidth product.

---

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Circuit description and identification of feedback.

In a CE amplifier, the emitter resistor $R_E$ can be either bypassed (using a large capacitor $C_E$) or **unbypassed**. When unbypassed, $R_E$ introduces **current-series negative feedback** because:
- The emitter current $I_E \approx I_C$ flows through $R_E$, developing a voltage $V_E = I_E \cdot R_E$ proportional to the **output current** (current sampling).
- This voltage $V_E$ appears in **series** with the base-emitter junction, opposing the input signal $V_{in}$ (series mixing).

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Small-signal analysis assumptions. With $r_o \to \infty$ (neglect Early effect for simplicity) and $h_{oe} \to 0$:

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Derive the closed-loop voltage gain.

The small-signal gain without feedback (with $R_E$ considered part of the basic amplifier input) is:

$$A_v = \frac{-\beta_{ac} R_C}{r_\pi + (\beta_{ac} + 1) R_E}$$

With feedback factor $\beta_v = R_E / R_C$ (effectively), the closed-loop gain becomes:

$$A_{vf} = \frac{A_v}{1 + A_v \beta_v} \approx \frac{-R_C}{R_E} \quad \text{(for deep feedback, } A_v \beta_v \gg 1\text{)}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Derive input resistance.

$$R_{if} = R_i (1 + A_v \beta_v) = [r_\pi + (\beta_{ac} + 1) R_E](1 + A_v \beta_v)$$

This shows that $R_{if}$ is much larger than the basic CE input resistance.

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Derive output resistance.

$$R_{of} = R_o (1 + A_v \beta_v) = R_C (1 + A_v \beta_v)$$

This shows that the output resistance **increases** with feedback, which is characteristic of **current sampling**.

---

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Amount of feedback.

$$1 + A_v \beta = 1 + (500)(0.02) = 1 + 10 = 11$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Closed-loop gain.

$$A_{vf} = \frac{500}{11} \approx 45.45$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Input and output resistances with feedback.

$$R_{if} = R_i (1 + A_v \beta) = 1.5 \text{ k}\Omega \times 11 = 16.5 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_{of} = R_o (1 + A_v \beta) = 5 \text{ k}\Omega \times 11 = 55 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** New cutoff frequencies.

$$f_{Lf} = \frac{f_L}{1 + A_v \beta} = \frac{50 \text{ Hz}}{11} \approx 4.55 \text{ Hz}$$

$$f_{Hf} = f_H (1 + A_v \beta) = 25 \text{ kHz} \times 11 = 275 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Comment on gain-bandwidth product.

$$GBP_{open} = 500 \times 25 \text{ kHz} = 12.5 \text{ MHz}$$

$$GBP_{closed} = 45.45 \times 275 \text{ kHz} = 12.5 \text{ MHz}$$

The **Gain-Bandwidth Product is conserved** — a fundamental invariant of single-pole negative feedback systems. The bandwidth has increased from 25 kHz to 275 kHz (× 11), and the gain has decreased by the same factor.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Mixing up topologies:** Students frequently confuse voltage-series with current-series, especially regarding output resistance behavior. **Voltage-series → $R_{of}$ decreases; Current-series → $R_{of}$ increases.** KTU examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the factor (1 + Aβ) in bandwidth calculations:** Students often compute new $f_H$ or $f_L$ but use the wrong direction. The upper cutoff **increases** and the lower cutoff **decreases** — not the other way around.
> 
> 3. **Not stating assumptions:** Always explicitly state that the feedback is **negative** (so 1+Aβ > 1) and that the analysis assumes a **single-pole** open-loop response.
> 
> 4. **Sign errors in voltage gain:** The closed-loop gain of an inverting CE stage is **negative**. Forgetting the negative sign costs 0.5 to 1 mark in numerical problems.
> 
> 5. **Mixing up input/output mixing types:** Series mixing = KVL; Shunt mixing = KCL. Mixing these up leads to wrong expressions for $R_{if}$ and $R_{of}$.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting to compute GBP verification:** KTU examiners love to ask students to verify the GBP invariance. Skipping this loses 1 mark.
> 
> 7. **Unit errors:** Always express frequencies in Hz, kHz, or MHz consistently. Mixing units (e.g., kHz with MHz) without conversion is a guaranteed 0.5-mark deduction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Master Equation (Memorize First):** $A_f = A / (1 + A\beta)$
- **Desensitivity Factor D = 1 + Aβ:** Always positive for negative feedback; always greater than 1.
- **Voltage-Series Topology:** $R_{if} = R_i(1+A\beta)$ ↑ ; $R_{of} = R_o/(1+A\beta)$ ↓ ; $A_{vf} = A_v/(1+A\beta)$
- **Current-Series Topology:** $R_{if} = R_i(1+A\beta)$ ↑ ; $R_{of} = R_o(1+A\beta)$ ↑ ; $G_{mf} = G_m/(1+A\beta)$
- **BJT Realization (Current-Series):** Common-Emitter stage with **unbypassed** $R_E$ — the voltage across $R_E$ is proportional to output current.
- **BJT Realization (Voltage-Series):** Emitter follower with output voltage divider — sampled output voltage.
- **Bandwidth Extension:** $f_{Hf} = f_H(1+A\beta)$ (increases); $f_{Lf} = f_L/(1+A\beta)$ (decreases)
- **Gain-Bandwidth Product:** Invariant in single-pole feedback systems: $GBP_{open} = GBP_{closed}$
- **Distortion Reduction:** $D_f = D_o / (1+A\beta)$ — feedback suppresses nonlinearity.
- **Sensitivity:** $\frac{dA_f}{A_f} = \frac{1}{1+A\beta} \cdot \frac{dA}{A}$ — fractional gain change is reduced by (1+Aβ).
- **Noise Consideration:** Feedback suppresses noise generated **inside the loop** but does NOT suppress input-referred noise.
- **Loop Stability Caveat:** Excessive feedback can cause **oscillation** if the loop phase shift reaches 180° before the gain drops below unity (Barkhausen criterion). This is why op-amps have **phase compensation capacitors**.
- **KTU Lab Viva Favorite:** "What happens if $\beta$ is increased?" — Answer: Loop gain increases, gain decreases, bandwidth increases, impedances shift, distortion decreases, but **stability margin reduces**.
- **Loop Gain Definition:** $T = A\beta$ — the gain product around the feedback loop. Always $T \gg 1$ in deep negative feedback.
- **Return Ratio:** The loop gain measured by breaking the loop and injecting a test signal — used in Middlebrook's method for rigorous feedback analysis.
- **Miller's Theorem Application:** Used to find the input/output resistances in CE stages with feedback, splitting $R_E$ or $R_F$ into input-side and output-side equivalents.
- **Assumption for All KTU Problems:** The feedback network is unilateral (β is independent of load and source), passive, and reciprocal.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Low Frequency Oscillators - RC Phase Shift or Wien Bridge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL308 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUIT AND LOGIC DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Low Frequency Oscillators - RC Phase Shift or Wien Bridge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Low Frequency Oscillator?

A **low frequency oscillator (LFO)** is an electronic circuit that generates a periodic, repeating waveform (typically sinusoidal) at frequencies in the range of **1 Hz to 100 kHz**. These oscillators are widely used as laboratory signal sources, in audio applications, function generators, and instrumentation systems where high-purity sine waves at low frequencies are required.

In the context of KTU PCBRL308, this experiment focuses on **RC-based sinusoidal oscillators**, where the frequency of oscillation is determined by **resistor (R) and capacitor (C) values** rather than by inductors (which would make it an LC oscillator suitable for higher frequencies).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *An oscillator is a circuit that converts DC energy from the supply into AC energy at a specific frequency without requiring an external input signal, sustained by positive feedback satisfying the **Barkhausen Criterion**.*

## 1.2 The Two Variants Studied in This Experiment

| Oscillator Type | Amplifier Used | Phase Shift by RC Network | Frequency Range |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **RC Phase Shift Oscillator** | Inverting (CE BJT / Op-Amp) | 180° from 3 RC sections | **1 Hz – 100 kHz** |
| **Wien Bridge Oscillator** | Non-Inverting (Op-Amp) | 0° from lead-lag network | **1 Hz – 1 MHz** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Swinging Pendulum

Imagine a grandfather clock's pendulum: once you give it a small push, the escapement mechanism provides a tiny push at just the right moment during each swing. The pendulum doesn't need a continuous push — it only needs a *timed, in-phase nudge* to keep going indefinitely.

An oscillator works exactly like this:
- The **amplifier** provides the energy (the "push").
- The **RC feedback network** decides the **timing** (when and how much phase shift to give back).
- The **Barkhausen criterion** ensures the push is always in the right direction at the right time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** An oscillator is essentially a tuned amplifier with a portion of its output cleverly fed back to its input in the correct phase, so the circuit "ticks" itself forever.

## 1.4 The Barkhausen Criterion (Foundation Concept)

For sustained oscillations, two simultaneous conditions must be met:

1. **Loop Gain Magnitude Condition:** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ — the product of amplifier gain (A) and feedback factor ($\beta$) must equal exactly **unity**.
2. **Loop Phase Condition:** $\angle A\beta = 0° \text{ (or } 360°\text{)}$ — the total phase shift around the loop must be **zero (or an integer multiple of 360°)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At the moment of switch-on, the loop gain must be slightly **greater than 1** (e.g., $|A\beta| = 1.01$) so that the oscillations build up. Once the desired amplitude is reached, automatic amplitude stabilization (AGC) reduces the effective gain back to **exactly 1**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 RC Phase Shift Oscillator — Operational Logic

The RC phase shift oscillator uses an **inverting amplifier** (CE BJT configuration or inverting op-amp) which inherently provides a **180° phase shift** between input and output. To satisfy the Barkhausen phase condition, the **RC feedback network** must contribute an **additional 180°**.

This 180° is obtained by cascading **three identical RC sections**, each contributing **60°** at the desired frequency of oscillation.

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic

- **Step 1:** A common-emitter (CE) amplifier provides a 180° phase inversion. This is its natural behavior — output is anti-phase to input.
- **Step 2:** The output of the amplifier is fed back to the input via a three-stage RC ladder network. Each stage is a high-pass filter (series C, shunt R).
- **Step 3:** At the **frequency of oscillation**, each RC section introduces a 60° phase lag, totaling 180°.
- **Step 4:** Total loop phase = 180° (amplifier) + 180° (RC network) = 360° ≡ 0° → Barkhausen satisfied.
- **Step 5:** For the magnitude condition, the **minimum closed-loop gain** of the CE stage must be **29** to compensate for the attenuation ($\vert\beta\vert = 1/29$) of the RC ladder.

## 2.2 Wien Bridge Oscillator — Operational Logic

The Wien bridge oscillator uses a **non-inverting amplifier** (op-amp) which provides **0° phase shift** by default. The frequency-selective feedback network must therefore contribute **0° (or 360°)** at the desired frequency.

The Wien network is a **lead-lag circuit** composed of:
- **Series arm:** $R$ in series with $C$ → behaves as a lead network at high frequencies.
- **Shunt arm:** $R$ in parallel with $C$ → behaves as a lag network at high frequencies.

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic

- **Step 1:** A non-inverting op-amp (with gain $A = 1 + R_f/R_i$) provides 0° phase shift.
- **Step 2:** The Wien network is a frequency-selective voltage divider. The series arm ($Z_s$) and shunt arm ($Z_p$) form an AC bridge.
- **Step 3:** At the **resonant frequency** $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$, the reactance of C equals R, and the bridge is **balanced**.
- **Step 4:** The feedback fraction is $\beta = 1/3$ at this frequency, requiring a non-inverting gain of **exactly 3** to satisfy $\vert A\beta\vert = 1$.
- **Step 5:** Amplitude stabilization is achieved using **non-linear elements** (back-to-back Zener diodes, thermistor, or JFET) in the feedback path.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | RC Phase Shift Oscillator | Wien Bridge Oscillator |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Frequency of Oscillation** | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ |
| 2 | **Angular Frequency** | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}}$ | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ |
| 3 | **Feedback Factor** $\beta$ | $\dfrac{1}{29}$ | $\dfrac{1}{3}$ |
| 4 | **Minimum Loop Gain** $\vert A\beta\vert = 1$ | $\vert A_v\vert = 29$ | $\vert A_v\vert = 3$ |
| 5 | **Phase Shift by RC Network** | $180°$ (3 × 60°) | $0°$ |
| 6 | **Phase Shift by Amplifier** | $180°$ (Inverting CE) | $0°$ (Non-Inverting) |
| 7 | **Design Constraint** | $R_C \geq 29 R_E$ for BJT CE | $R_f = 2 R_i$ for op-amp |
| 8 | **Stability of Frequency** | Moderate (depends on 6 RC values) | High (depends on only 2 RC values) |
| 9 | **Typical Frequency Range** | **1 Hz – 100 kHz** | **1 Hz – 1 MHz** |
